sections.gist: Introduction and origin: maiden name, village near Novo Mesto, and the 1920 departure
Family home and farm: parents' occupations, peasant house with attached barn, animals, and food preservation
Family and childhood: mother as widow, siblings, sister's death, and the walk to school
Wartime village life: hosting refugees, washing clothes at the river, hospitals, and languages spoken
Political changes and bartering: shift from Austria-Hungary to Yugoslavia, city-country bartering, and river crossings
Preparing to emigrate: father's letters, paperwork and approvals, brothers' fates, and feelings about leaving
The journey: train to Trieste, ship conditions and food, arrival in New York, and train to Chicago and Utah
Arrival in Utah: why father lived in coal-mining towns, meeting him in Price, going to Sunnyside, and starting school
Utah years and the Depression: school progress, mother's adjustment, father's failing health, and the move to Colorado
Marriage and Denver: 1927 wedding, the Model T trip, and the Slovenian community in Denver
Depression-era work and citizenship: packing house, daughter Mitzi, becoming citizens in 1932, and pride in America

sentences.text: PHILLIPS:	This Andrew Phillips.
It's Monday the 28th of August, 1989.
I'm with Mary Grande, spelled G-R-A-N-D-E.  This is Interview Number 414 [DP-40] of the Ellis Island Oral History Project.
Commencing this interview at a little bit before 12:30.
Mrs. Grande, could you start by telling me what your name is and where you immigrated from.
GRANDE:	What my maiden name was?
PHILLIPS:	Uh, let me just get the microphone set up.
That's fine.
That's fine.
GRANDE:	My maiden name was, uh, Marija Yankovik.
And we came, my mother and I came from Kandijaright over Novo Mesto, which is in Yugoslavia now.
Originally it was Austria/Hungary.
And we came here, we left there August 12, 1920 and we stayed a few days in Trieste, Italy.
PHILLIPS:	Can you spell that for me, please?
GRANDE:	T-R-I-E-S-T-E, Italy.
And then we got on the ship.
I believe the name of it was America.
And it was right after World War One, so they stopped it in a lot of places.
In London, and I suppose Paris, and all over.
But part of our clothes was stolen in Trieste, so a fellow that my mother knew, he went in, I believe, in London, to buy some clothes for us.
He could speak American.
He came back from the States and who he bought back I don't remember.
But, uh, he bought some clothes so we had something to change in to, to wear.
PHILLIPS:	Before you tell us about travelling from your home country to the United States, could you tell us a little bit about what your home was like.
For instance, start by telling us what your parents did for a living.
GRANDE:	Well, my father was here in the States since 1911.
In 		fact, he was, during World War one here in the United States, and my mother was a farmer, and she raised some cattle and bartered with them.
PHILLIPS:	Tell me what your home was like.
What did it look like?
GRANDE:	It was an average peasant home.
It was three rooms, living room and kitchen in the middle and a small room on the side.
And then facing what would be, I guess, the east side was half, was a barn where the cattle were.
PHILLIPS:	So the cattle were actually, they were part of the 		house.
The barn was part of the house.
GRANDE:	Yes.
PHILLIPS:	Explain that to us, what was that like.

GRANDE:	Well, it was all one building and there were, later on they made that as all one room, but at that time we had a half a room was like for the barn, for the cows, or when my brother had horses it was there.
And then there was a manure pile just to the side of it.
And the majority of people had it similar like that.
PHILLIPS:	Why were the houses and the barns constructed together 		like that?
GRANDE:	It's been historical, as far as I know.
I don't know any different.
They don't do it now any more like that, but they used to.
PHILLIPS:	And what was it like for a little girl to be living so close to the animals?

Did you enjoy that, or were you--

	GRANDE:	Oh, yes.
I enjoyed it because they were part of my life.
In fact, I had a pet cow that I loved very much.
And then the pigs were, the pig sty was just across from the kitchen door, just a little ways away.
So all the animals were right close to the house.
It was just a regular home life between the animals.
You wouldn't bring them in the house, but you had them outside.
PHILLIPS:	So you could, when you were sitting down to eat, for instance, you could look out the kitchen door and there were the pigs.
GRANDE:	Right.
PHILLIPS:	And you had names for the pigs?
GRANDE:	Sometimes.
PHILLIPS:	Did you like the pigs?
GRANDE:	Oh, yeah.
They used to slaughter them in winter and save some meat, cured it, for the summer.
And some of it was for right away.
Not too much fresh, but mostly cured.
PHILLIPS:	How did they cure it?
GRANDE:	Uh, salt and water and pack it, put some rocks on it and 		let it stay like that and then they would smoke it.
PHILLIPS:	Did you used to help do that?
GRANDE:	I had to help with, when they killed the pigs I had to catch the blood.
I didn't like it, but that was part of my job.
PHILLIPS:	Can you perhaps remember actually what you did?
Can you 		describe that for us?
GRANDE:	Well, when my mother got the fellow to kill the pig, well, I had to hold the pan there and catch the blood, because they made the blood sausage out of that.
Of course, they worked with it to make it good, and then they made the blood sausage and usually they fried the liver right away, good and fresh.
That was really good.
Then later, out of the other leftover pieces of regular meat they'd make smoked sausage and they'd smoke them and have them for during the summer.
They had for special occasions, or when they had hired help.
They had different pieces for different times.
PHILLIPS:	So you got a lot of different, uh, meats.
You got your money's worth out of those little pigs across the way from the kitchen.
GRANDE:	Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
And then, of course, we had 		the milk from the cows.
We didn't kill the cows at home.
It's very seldom we had beef but, uh, we chickens, so there was chicken meat and eggs and pigs and milk and cheese, whatever.
(Sound of door opening in the background.)
MITZI STACKHOUSE:  Hello.
GRANDE:	Hi.
PHILLIPS:	Just a small interruption there.
Clyde and Mitzi 		Stackhouse.

Mitzi is the daughter--

	GRANDE:  Yeah.
PHILLIPS:	--of Mrs. Grande.
Okay.
We were back, you were telling 		us a little bit about your life on your farm.
Can you tell us, perhaps, how you warmed the place in the wintertime.
What, was it cold for you?
GRANDE:	Well, we, uh, heated it with the wood.
There was times that my mother later on got some coal, but not very much.
It was mostly wood.
And the forest wasn't far from the house, so we used to get the wood there.
And, of course, she had quite a bit hauled in the fall, so we would have wood.
PHILLIPS:	You say hauled in the fall, she got the wood during the 		fall?
GRANDE:	Yeah, so we'd have it all winter long.
But she would cut it up herself, saw and chop it and, of course, later on when I was bigger I tried to help, which I wasn't much good.
But I helped on the farm what little I could, and I had a mile or better to go to school.
PHILLIPS:	Before you tell us about going to school, can you tell 		me what your life was like for your mother.
She was, just one moment.
We need for you to be as quiet as possible (addressing daughter and son-in-law).
GRANDE:	Well, originally she was a widow and she married her husband.
And then she bought this home.
And then when she met--  (Break in tape.)
PHILLIPS:	Okay, Mrs. Grande, could you continue please?
I think we were just talking about how difficult it was for your mother.
You said she was a widow and married again.
GRANDE:	She was a widow, and she had two boys.
One died before, 		but two of them were living, so she was raising them.
And then I came along after she married with my husband, uh, my father.
And, uh, he came to the States, and there was another sister born after that.
PHILLIPS:	Could you tell me, I mean, is sounds like your mother must have been a tremendously strong and hardy woman to look after a farm and bring up these children and chop the wood.
Was that normal?
GRANDE:	Normal, normal.
Most all the women did similar work, 		worked on the farm and just took care of everything.
There was quite a few men that came to the States to make a livelihood and then probably come back, which a lot of them never did.
A lot of them took their family over here, but a lot of them, some did help.
But during the wartime they couldn't help back and forth at all.
So my mother used to kind of sell milk and eggs and whatever she could, products.
Not too much off the farm because that was our livelihood throughout the year.
And you had to keep some for the seeds for next year.
PHILLIPS:	Did you tell me how ,any were in your family?
Could you 		tell me that?
GRANDE:	Well, there was, actually, uh, four, five, with my mother.
And then my sister died.
Oh, she was about a year old when she died, so there was four of us.
And, uh, we'd done the best we could, just like a lot of others.
PHILLIPS:	Why did your sister die?
What did she die of?
GRANDE:	Childhood problems.
PHILLIPS:	Was that very common in your village?
GRANDE:	Well, there was quite a few children that did die.
I don't know exactly what the cause or what it was, but she died.
PHILLIPS:	Now, could you tell me, please, about going to school.
You said you had to walk to school.
Tell me about your school days.
GRANDE:	Well, it was a good mile, or maybe further, past the cemetery, which I wasn't scared of.
I didn't mind it a bit.
And we crossed the track.
And, of course, one time I didn't pay attention, the weather as bad and I had my head down, the rails were down that the train was going to come,and I bumped in there, which wasn't very good.
But that woke me up to pay attention.
But, uh, we walked back and forth.
We didn't think nothing of it.
That was normal.
Nobody had cars or anything, and they didn't take them with the wagon because there was too much other work to be done.
And then when my brother got older, he was quite a bit older than me, well, uh, he went and stayed with my aunt for a while on a different part of the area.
And so my mother and I were alone for quite a while.
And then she bartered with the cattle, and sometimes I'd stay with the neighbors overnight when she'd go and barter to different places.
And all that she done, all of that walking.
She didn't have no other transportation.
So then after the war, when my dad started writing about coming to the States, naturally we were all enthused about it.
I don't know if my mother was enthused or not, but I was.
PHILLIPS:	Tell me what it was like for you during the war.
Do you 		have any memories of that?
GRANDE:	Well, we had to have refugees from the Italian side, and 		the extra room that we had that was like, it could have been a summer kitchen.
Well, we had to give that to the refugees and everybody had to share their home with the refugees.
And, of course, they spoke quite a bit Italian, but we managed to understand each other pretty good.
PHILLIPS:	Do you know, or do you remember, whether it was the government that forced you to take the refugees, or the people in the community just did it?
GRANDE:	It was more or less a government decision that everybody 		would take some refugees.
And we went down by the river which was about a good half-a-mile or further to wash clothes on the rocks.
I don't know if you ever heard of it or not, but that was, and then my mother would take them sometimes over to the hospital which was the other way.
It wasn't as far.
The only thing, she didn't like to go there because there was too much bloody stuff in there.
PHILLIPS:	Why?
GRANDE:	Uh, to wash clothes in shelter instead of outdoors.
PHILLIPS:	But you said there was some bloody stuff in there.
Why?
GRANDE:	Well.
it was a hospital, and they'd let women come there 		and wash clothes but naturally the hospital supplies were going through first.
PHILLIPS:	So the rubbish and the effluent from the hospital was 		thrown into the river.
GRANDE:	Yeah.
PHILLIPS:	And so you probably tried to wash upstream from the 		hospital.
GRANDE:	Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
Definitely.
And there was two hospitals.
One on one side of the river was for men, and the one on the other side of the river was for women.
They didn't have them all combined together like they have over here now.
They have them like that now too over there.
But at that time there it was each separate.
And there was a castle right across the street from the hospital, which is now tuberculosis sanitarium.
They don't have no castles no more.
So there's quite a bit of changes any more, too.
PHILLIPS:	But in those days who lived in that castle?
GRANDE:	The Austrian people.
They were the higher-ups from the Austrian, not government, but they were well-to-do and all that.

But--

	PHILLIPS:	What language did you speak?
GRANDE:	Actually Slovanian, and we had to take German.
PHILLIPS:	Not Hungarian.
GRANDE:	No, no.
German was our main language, like in office or 		different places, you almost had to learn how to speak German to get along with them.
PHILLIPS:	That was the official language.
GRANDE:	Right.
PHILLIPS:	So how many languages were spoken, then, in your 		village?
GRANDE:	The two.
German and Slovanian.

PHILLIPS:	Do you remember much, at that age, about any political 		structures or political problems, or--

	GRANDE:	All I kind of remember when, I guess it was about 1918 when they was going to overthrow Austria/Hungary altogether and change it to Yugoslavia.
PHILLIPS:	When who was?

GRANDE:	Well, the government was a changing deal because the--

	PHILLIPS:	I'm sorry, which government are you talking about?
GRANDE:	Part of Yugoslavia.
And the First World War started 		down in Sarajevo in what is now Yugoslavia.
It's down more or less in the Serbian section.
That's where Ferdinand got killed and World War One started.
and I kind of remember when they had the meetings around 1918 and '19, '20 and after all that, well, it became Yugoslavia.
So actually I was born in Austria/Hungary and came here from Yugoslavia.
PHILLIPS:	Do you remember what your older relatives, or your mother, or people who came to your house, were talking about during those times?
Were they afraid?
Were they excited?
What was the atmosphere like?
GRANDE:	Well, during the war they were excited and all that but, 		uh, not too much, because you just had to go along.
There were restrictions on certain things you couldn't buy.
And, uh, the people that lived in the city, which was across the river from us, they didn't have food, and we had more food because we were out in the country.
So for any small amount of food, either eggs, or chicken, or no matter what, or milk, they would share clothing because they had plenty of clothes, but they had a hard time getting food.
PHILLIPS:	And so that was, did that become a barter system that 		you, part of the barter system you were describing before, or not?
Or did they pay money for the food?
GRANDE:	Oh, no.
It would be a bartering.
I had, I usually had real nice clothes and, uh, when we were coming here to the States I'd have had beautiful clothes if it wouldn't be stolen in Italy while we were waiting for the ship.
We had to stay there three days and they had us, they had my mother put the suitcases in the storage, and when she went to get them they were gone.
Not only hers, a lot of others too.
PHILLIPS:	Meanwhile, back before that happened, how did the people 		cross that river?

Was there a boat, or a bridge, or--

	GRANDE:	Oh, further down there was a big ridge.
There was, uh, or they went with wagons and whatever needed to be.
Of course, you don't see the wagons now any more.
All you see is cars.
But at that time there was wagons.
PHILLIPS:	When was it that you first learned that you were going 		to travel to America?
GRANDE:	Oh, it was, oh, I guess about, not the very first part, 		but about towards the second part of 1920.
PHILLIPS:	And tell me how you learned about that?
GRANDE:	Well, my mother was telling me about the letters she got 		from my father, and then he sent us the tickets.
And she had to go to different places to get it approved and all that.
But, uh, not too many.
Like some of them had to go through more than she did.
And I didn't have to go anywhere because I was a minor.
So, but on the passport, uh, her and my pictures were together, so everything went, whatever she got, it was for me, too.
PHILLIPS:	And how about the other children?
GRANDE:	Well, my oldest brother, he had to go in the army.
See, 		they have to serve three years all the time.
But, uh, he had to go in.
And then the other one, they was going to bring him here.
And in the spring, when he was supposed to go kind of like to report for the army, he was on the wagon and he fell or jumped or whatever, and he got killed.
So then this one got to come from the army earlier to take over the farm.
PHILLIPS:	Because his brother had been killed.
GRANDE:	Yeah.
PHILLIPS:	And so did your brother accompany you to the United 		States?
GRANDE:	No.
He would have to come after it.
It was just my 		mother and I that came then.
PHILLIPS:	All right.
Now, tell me what you actually had to, to do 		to get your papers.

GRANDE:	Well, I wasn't with my mother too much, but she had to go through quite a bit of different courts and different offices and get approvals of, oh, different higher-ups that were in line with the travelling and all.
So she had to make quite a few different trips.
PHILLIPS:	Do you know how long it took her to get the approval?
GRANDE:	Oh, it was about, the last month before we left it seemed like she was always coming or going to different places.
PHILLIPS:	And meanwhile your father was in America?
GRANDE:	Yeah.
PHILLIPS:	So tell me about finally leaving.
How did you feel?
GRANDE:	Oh, I was anxious just to get here to meet my dad.
PHILLIPS:	Weren't you going to miss your friends?
GRANDE:	I didn't think about it then.
PHILLIPS:	How old were you when you were leaving?
GRANDE: 	Ten.
I was just anxious to meet my dad.
PHILLIPS:	And how had your school life been?

Had you enjoyed 		school back in--

	GRANDE:	Well, I like school.
I had average grades.
I wouldn't say they were exceptional, but I had them average, and I had to take German for two years in third and fourth grade.
Of course, Later on when I came here there was no German people around, so I just kind of forgot it.
But, uh, it would be nice if I had remembered it.
PHILLIPS:	So how did you, you got to Trieste, think you described.
GRANDE:	We went by train.
We went by train, and then we got to 		where we had, where my mother checked in the suitcases and, uh, we just had like a barn place.
It wasn't a hotel or anything, where we stayed for, till 15th.
And then we went on the ship.

And, uh, my dad paid for first class but they put us down on the bottom and then this one fellow spoke up that was, uh, here before, and he seen my mother's card, and he said, "You don't belong down here.
You should go up higher."
And we had mostly fish and macaroni on the ship, which I didn't like very well, but you had to eat something.
PHILLIPS:	So did you manage to get up to first class?
GRANDE:	Not first.
But we got up to second.
We got up to second class.
It was better, but nothing to brag about compared to what the ships are now.
PHILLIPS:	Did you still have to eat fish and macaroni?
GRANDE:	Oh, yes.
I guess that was the basic, it was an Italian ship, so I guess that's what it was.

And they stopped it in London, they stopped it in different places until finally I guess they got a call from the United States where the people are that were supposed to be in by a certain time and they weren't, and so the ship started going real fast, and finally got to New York.
PHILLIPS:	Do you remember that?
GRANDE:	Well, I do some, not too much.
I suppose I was too excited about getting to my dad's place.
I know there was quite a few that had eye disease and they had them there for a few days, and then a lot of them were sent back from where they were coming from, either Italy, Germany or Yugoslavia.

And then uh, well, we were there, and they vaccinated us again and checked us through and all, and finally we got to go and I think we changed trains in Chicago, from New York to Chicago, and then changed trains.
And there were some people that went here to Denver that were from more or less the same area as we were.
But, uh, we got on a different train in Chicago.
The train they got was different than the one we had.
And that's the first time I got to see bananas, on the train.
And, of course, there was a fellow that, he went over there to get his two children, and he didn't buy them very much.
Whatever, the rest of them, like my mother and this other lady bought, we shared with his kids.
They didn't like it very much, but what can you do.
So we all went, there was a lady with her two daughters, and this man with his two children, and my mother and me, we all went to the same town in Utah.
We got off in Price, and we, of course, that's where my dad met us, and then we had to take the bus twenty-eight miles to Sunnyside.
PHILLIPS:	Why was it that you decided to move up to, you were on 		your way to Denver, I take it?
GRANDE:	No, no.
We were on the way to Utah to begin with.
That's where my dad lived.
PHILLIPS:	Why had he chosen to live in Utah?
GRANDE:	Well, he was working in coal mines.
He worked in different mines in Utah, around.
He worked part-time years before that in  Aspen, Colorado, but he went back to Utah around Tooele.
And at that time he was in Sunnyside, and so we lived there about six and a half years, and then his health went bad, and so we came to Colorado.
PHILLIPS:	I see.
Can you tell me how you felt when you arrived in 		the United States and how eventually you must have started school, what was that like for you?
GRANDE:	Well, we lived, my dad rented a company four room house that was a double, and the other family was similar in nationality as we were.
And so, uh, the kids could talk broken Slovanian, so we kind of got started getting along, and I'd pick up words from them little by little.
And we came on a Thursday, and the following Monday I went to school.
And being that these were going to the same grades, so they put me in the fourth grade instead of the first.
And I struggle along pretty good because as far as figures you didn't have to talk.
You could just write, and it was good.
And, uh, so I was in a county contest the first and second year for arithmetic to where we went from Sunnyside to Price, we stayed overnight.
And it was good.
And then I skipped the sixth grade and in seventh grade I was in a running contest.
I wouldn't go now, but I did go then.
PHILLIPS:	Okay.
Let me just turn my tape over.
PHILLIPS:	This is side two, Interview Number 414 [DP-40], with Mary Grande.
Um, so, uh, you did fairly well at school?
GRANDE:	Yeah, I done, I'd say I wasn't the best, but I was with 		the top ones.
PHILLIPS:	Now, tell me what life was like now for your mother.
She no longer had all of those animals to look after.
It must have been very different.
What did she do?
GRANDE:	All we could do was housework and laundry, cleaning and cooking, and that.
So she got adjusted pretty well.

She always hoped to go back, but she never did because they started talking about the war and she didn't want to be in another war over there, which they had it worse right in our area in the second one than they did in the first.
And my brother, half-brother, of course, he lived there until the '70s when he died.
She gave the property over to him.
And they had pretty rough deals there during the second war.
In fact, one of his daughters got deaf and dumb on account of the noise of the cannons that they were shooting on their farm.
We were right close to where they had the battles and everything, my brother was, so.
But, uh, I got pretty well with the kids in school.
Of course, I couldn't speak, but I started learning very fast the best way I could and I enjoyed the school.
PHILLIPS:	And where were you living at this point?
GRANDE:	In Sunnyside, Utah.
PHILLIPS:	When did you, well, before I ask you that question, uh, can you tell me a little bit about the Depression years for your family.
What was that like?
GRANDE:	Well, the Depression really hit us after I came here to Colorado.
Right after I got married, that's when we had the worst Depression.

But, uh, dad was kind of, his health was going down great, so he couldn't, uh, work and he figured, well, between my mother and him, they figured maybe he could do some outside work over here, and we had, my mother's sister lived up on northeast of Greeley on what they call the dry land, so we were there for a week, and then we came to Denver.
So he got a job finally over at the smelter, and he worked there for a while, but couldn't work too long.
And then little by little he tried to work, he went finally back into the mine, and he couldn't work too long there.
Finally the doctor said he couldn't work no more.
PHILLIPS:	What was, uh, how did you feel, at this point, or how did your family feel about being so far away from home?

Did they miss--

	GRANDE:	My mother missed it a lot.
But, uh, I'm sorry to say, I 		didn't		.
PHILLIPS:	So you got married.
When did you get married?
GRANDE:	In 1927, here in Denver.
And I've been here ever since.
PHILLIPS:	And, uh, you moved to Denver when, from Sunnyside?
GRANDE;	In 1926, in October.
We drove a Model T.  In fact, I drove it most of the way, and we stopped in Aspen.
I had an uncle, my mother's brother, there.
We stopped there for a month, and then we came on to Colorado.
And, uh, after a while we both got jobs, and it worked out.
And I met my husband on New Year's and married him in May.
He was from the same area as I was, and years later we went back there.
He liked the area where I was raised much better than where he was because it was close to the city.
And him and I went back there three times, one by car and twice with the plane.
And, of course, I've been there since a few times.
PHILLIPS:	Why was it that there were so many people from your, 		from that area that you came from in Europe, living in this area around Denver?
Do you know?
GRANDE:	Well, it's a settlement.
When a group gets settled and then somebody else tries to come in, and they just like to integrate together.
PHILLIPS:	Okay.
Is there anything else that you'd like to, think 		we might be interested to hear about?
GRANDE:	Well, I worked later on.
During the Depression it was kind of rough.
My husband wasn't making much.
Worked every other week and the girls were getting to the point, being in school, we sent them to parochial school.
And naturally we had to pay.
It wasn't like now that somebody else pays for them a lot.
And then, uh, U finally got a job in a packing house, which I worked close to thirty years in it.
And in between every so often I had to have different surgeries and I'd be home.
PHILLIPS:	What kind of work, excuse me, what kind of work 		particularly were you doing?
GRANDE:	Cutting up meat, pork and beef.
Cut it for scraps for 		sausage and stuff.

And then later on she came into the packing house and she worked with me for a while, and then she went down in the smokehouse where they have to smoke bacon and ham, and she worked there until she had to give up medically on account of it.
PHILLIPS:	Let me explain for identification who she is.
GRANDE:	My daughter, Mitzi.
PHILLIPS:	M-I-T-Z-I.  Mitzi Stackhouse.
GRANDE:	Yeah.
I have another daughter, Jenny Musk.
She's a 		nurse.
She lives way up in Northglen.
PHILLIPS:	Okay.
Unless there's something else you think you'd 		like to us all.
GRANDE:	Well, we became, my husband and I became American citizens in 1932 and we tried to vote every time ever since.
Maybe not right, but we try our best.
And every time I go back to Europe I'm happy to come back.
This is my country.

codes.label: Place of origin anchored to shifting imperial borders
codes.definition: The speaker locates her village within a political geography that has changed (Austria-Hungary then Yugoslavia), tying personal identity to the dissolution of the prewar empire.
codes.model_rationale: The speaker must gloss her village by reference to successive political formations, indicating that origin is narrated through geopolitical rupture rather than a stable homeland.

codes.label: Material precarity and theft during transit
codes.definition: Travelers arrived at a port with diminished belongings, having lost part of their clothing, leaving them without essentials for the remainder of the voyage.
codes.model_rationale: The theft of clothes is presented as a concrete vulnerability that required outside remedy, marking the transit as materially insecure.

codes.label: Transnational family coordination across borders
codes.definition: Emigration is organized as a distributed effort between an absent father in the destination country and a mother handling logistics in the origin country, with letters and prepaid tickets initiating the process.
codes.model_rationale: The section repeatedly frames the move as a multi-country project: father in America sending tickets and information, mother on the ground executing the steps.

codes.label: Anticipatory joy centered on paternal reunion
codes.definition: The child's emotional orientation toward emigration is dominated by eagerness to meet the father, eclipsing awareness of what is being left behind.
codes.model_rationale: Several sentences express the ten-year-old's single-minded focus on reunification, framing emigration through longing for the father.

codes.label: Paternal itinerant labor in extractive industries
codes.definition: Father's pattern of moving among mining sites across states (Aspen, Tooele, Sunnyside) tied to coal-mine employment.
codes.model_rationale: Captures the cross-state trajectory of the father's mining work as an analytic pattern rather than a travel itinerary.

codes.label: Relocation triggered by paternal health decline
codes.definition: Mobility decisions are forced by the working-age father's deteriorating health, ending the mining-town chapter.
codes.model_rationale: Identifies bodily breakdown of the breadwinner as the structural cause of the next move, not preference.

codes.label: Peer-mediated English acquisition
codes.definition: Initial English vocabulary is picked up informally from co-ethnic neighbor children rather than taught in school.
codes.model_rationale: Codes the social mechanism by which language was acquired, treating peer children as linguistic intermediaries.

codes.label: Compressed school entry and accelerated grade placement
codes.definition: Rapid enrollment after arrival and placement above the expected grade level, assuming equivalence with US peers.
codes.model_rationale: Surfaces the school's institutional logic of rapid assimilation and age-based grade assignment despite limited English.

codes.label: Kin-based resettlement infrastructure
codes.definition: Reliance on existing family members (mother's sister) as an initial foothold and staging point in the new region.
codes.model_rationale: Captures the social architecture of chain migration rather than the literal itinerary.

codes.label: Staged, multi-port post-war transatlantic passage
codes.definition: The voyage was not direct but involved an intermediate land stay and a ship that called at multiple ports, characteristic of disrupted early-1920s shipping.
codes.model_rationale: The ship stopping 'in a lot of places' (London, Paris, etc.) after World War One is named by the speaker as a feature of the journey, distinguishing this transit from later direct steerage passages.

codes.label: Reliance on a returning diaspora acquaintance for rescue
codes.definition: Mother and daughter depended on a man known to the mother — apparently a returnee from America — to intervene and restore what had been lost.
codes.model_rationale: The narrator frames recovery as contingent on a pre-existing personal connection to someone who had crossed the Atlantic in the opposite direction, illustrating how diasporic networks functioned during the journey itself.

codes.label: American English fluency as diasporic capital
codes.definition: The helpful acquaintance's value is explicitly located in his ability to 'speak American,' a competence tied to prior American residence that positioned him as an intermediary.
codes.model_rationale: Highlighting his command of 'American' rather than his nationality or kinship treats linguistic fluency as the operative resource that enabled rescue in a foreign port.

codes.label: Female kin unit navigating migration with constrained agency
codes.definition: A mother-and-daughter pair undertakes the voyage together; their capacity to act in transit depends on the initiative of a male acquaintance, suggesting gendered structures of early-twentieth-century female migration.
codes.model_rationale: The narrative centers a female traveling unit whose recovery from theft is performed by a man they happened to know, implicitly framing women's transatlantic mobility as mediated through male diasporic figures.

codes.label: Transnational paternal absence and mother-headed farm
codes.definition: The father's long-standing presence in the United States leaves the mother as the de facto operator of the rural household and farm, structuring the home the speaker describes.
codes.model_rationale: The father is introduced as already in the US since 1911, while the mother is identified as the farmer who raises and barters cattle; this reframes the 'home' as a mother-run unit and foreshadows the migration narrative.

codes.label: Integrated human-livestock dwelling as regional norm
codes.definition: The peasant house and barn are built as a single structure with animals immediately adjacent to domestic rooms, a layout the speaker describes as historically typical of the community.
codes.model_rationale: Multiple sentences describe the single-building layout, the attached byre and manure pile, and the speaker's insistence that 'the majority of people had it similar' and that it was a long-standing, inherited convention rather than an individual choice.

codes.label: Children's emotional proximity to farm animals
codes.definition: Livestock are framed not as utility objects but as everyday companions of childhood, with naming, affection, and the animals figured as part of 'regular home life'.
codes.model_rationale: The speaker describes a beloved pet cow, names for the pigs, and the animals as 'part of my life' and 'just a regular home life,' indicating an affective, relational orientation toward livestock.

codes.label: Seasonal slaughter and cured-meat provisioning cycle
codes.definition: Pigs are killed in winter, the meat is salt-cured, weighted, and smoked to provide preserved food through the following summer, embedding the household in an annual preservation calendar.
codes.model_rationale: The speaker links winter slaughter, the salt/water/rock cure, smoking, and the allocation of different cuts to different occasions, illustrating a deliberate temporal logic of food storage.

codes.label: Children's obligatory participation in slaughter and butchery
codes.definition: Children are expected to perform necessary but unpleasant tasks during animal slaughter — such as catching blood for blood sausage — as a normal part of their household labor.
codes.model_rationale: Repeated 'I had to' formulations and the phrase 'part of my job' mark these tasks as required subsistence labor transmitted across generations, even when the child dislikes them.

codes.label: Diversified mixed-livestock subsistence economy
codes.definition: The household draws food and exchange value from a portfolio of animals — cows for milk, cheese, and barter; pigs for fresh and preserved meat; chickens for eggs and meat — rather than reliance on a single commodity.
codes.model_rationale: Across the section the speaker enumerates cattle, pigs, and chickens each supplying different products, and notes beef is rare at home, capturing a multi-species household economy.

codes.label: Mother as farm operator and processor of food
codes.definition: The mother is the central agent who manages the farm, arranges hired labor for slaughter, and oversees the chain of preservation and cooking.
codes.model_rationale: Sentences attribute farming, bartering, and the coordination of slaughter and sausage-making to the mother, marking her as the operative head of the rural enterprise.

codes.label: Affective insider framing of farm life against outsider's utilitarian reading
codes.definition: While the speaker describes the home-and-barn arrangement as ordinary and beloved, the interviewer reframes it instrumentally as 'getting your money's worth'; the speaker's emphatic 'Oh, yes' resists that commodifying lens.
codes.model_rationale: The exchange between interviewer ('money's worth') and speaker (repetition of 'Oh, yes') captures a tension between an outside economistic view of peasant life and the speaker's embedded, affective belonging to it.

codes.label: State-mandated domestic hospitality for refugees
codes.definition: Government policy required civilian households to surrender private space (e.g., summer kitchens) to host refugees, translating state refugee policy into coerced household sharing.
codes.model_rationale: Captures the analytic point that refugee absorption was state-imposed rather than voluntary, with domestic interiors converted into reception space.

codes.label: Cross-language accommodation with displaced populations
codes.definition: Hosts and refugees negotiated mutual intelligibility despite speaking different languages, managing communication barriers through pragmatic rather than fluent contact.
codes.model_rationale: Highlights the everyday linguistic work required when linguistically distinct populations were forced into shared domestic space.

codes.label: Riverine domestic labor under wartime conditions
codes.definition: Women's routine household work (laundry) required travel to a distant river and washing on rocks, reflecting gendered division of domestic labor in a village without in-home facilities.
codes.model_rationale: Names the analytic concept of river-based laundering as gendered domestic infrastructure rather than logging the literal trip as an event.

codes.label: Environmental contamination from medical waste into shared water
codes.definition: Hospital effluent and waste entered the same river used for civilian laundry, creating a public-health hazard tied to the spatial co-location of care and domestic labor sites.
codes.model_rationale: Captures the structural overlap of medical and domestic water use and the resulting sanitary risk, including the mother's avoidance of the hospital due to its bloody environment.

codes.label: Strategic risk navigation in everyday practice
codes.definition: Households adapted routines to mitigate environmental hazards, here by deliberately washing upstream of the hospital discharge.
codes.model_rationale: Reads the upstream-washing practice as tacit risk literacy, an analytic concept about how civilians internalize and act on infrastructural hazards.

codes.label: Gender-segregated medical infrastructure
codes.definition: Hospitals were physically and organizationally separated by sex, with men's and women's facilities on opposite sides of the river rather than co-located.
codes.model_rationale: Documents the historical organization of care by gender and the speaker's awareness of this as a contrast to later consolidated arrangements.

codes.label: Imperial ruling class visible in the local landscape
codes.definition: Austrian elites (described as well-to-do rather than governmental) occupied prominent local structures such as the castle situated directly across from the hospital.
codes.model_rationale: Captures the spatial proximity of imperial elite residence to civilian and medical infrastructure as an analytic feature of Habsburg-era village life.

codes.label: Imperial linguistic hierarchy
codes.definition: Slovenian functioned as the domestic language while German was the imposed administrative/official language required for navigating offices and public institutions.
codes.model_rationale: Reads the bilingual situation as a hierarchy of imperial domination rather than neutral multilingualism, with German as the language of institutional access.

codes.label: Erosion of prewar architectural and institutional landscape
codes.definition: Prewar landmarks such as castles were repurposed into modern institutions (e.g., tuberculosis sanitarium), marking the disappearance of older structures under modernization.
codes.model_rationale: Frames the speaker's lament about vanishing castles as an analytic observation about the transformation of the built environment from imperial to modern use.

codes.label: Childhood peripheral awareness of regime change
codes.definition: Major geopolitical transitions (Austria-Hungary to Yugoslavia, c. 1918–1920) are recalled only vaguely and from a child's vantage, with the Sarajevo assassination framing the war's origins in now-Yugoslav territory.
codes.model_rationale: The speaker signals distance from formal politics through hedging ('all I kind of remember'), yet links the regime change to the Sarajevo trigger, showing how macro-history is absorbed by children as atmosphere rather than analysis.

codes.label: Resigned civilian compliance under war conditions
codes.definition: Ordinary people endure wartime with muted stoicism — excitement mixed with the recognition that one 'just had to go along' rather than resist.
codes.model_rationale: The phrase 'you just had to go along' captures a civilian posture of pragmatic acquiescence to forces beyond individual control, distinct from fear or enthusiasm.

codes.label: Wartime consumer restrictions
codes.definition: Formal wartime prohibitions on purchasing certain goods, signaling state-imposed scarcity.
codes.model_rationale: Cited as a defining feature of the war economy that pushed households toward non-market exchange.

codes.label: Rural-urban barter complementarity under scarcity
codes.definition: With formal markets constrained, countryside households (food surplus) exchange with city households (clothing surplus) across the river, sustaining both communities.
codes.model_rationale: The exchange of eggs/chicken/milk for clothing illustrates a structural complementarity: rural and urban households held inverted surpluses, making barter a rational adaptation to wartime scarcity.

codes.label: Pre-automotive river-crossing infrastructure
codes.definition: Trans-local mobility depended on a bridge and horse-drawn wagons, a transport regime the speaker marks as superseded by automobiles.
codes.model_rationale: The contrast between wagons (then) and cars (now) frames the village's link to the city as part of a vanishing pre-modern infrastructure, anchoring the barter system in physical proximity across the river.

codes.label: Bureaucratic navigation of emigration approvals
codes.definition: The emigration process required the mother to make repeated visits to multiple courts and offices to secure approvals from various authorities, intensified in the final month before departure.
codes.model_rationale: Multiple sentences detail the volume and variety of administrative touchpoints the mother had to clear, characterizing the move as procedurally demanding.

codes.label: Gendered burden of emigration paperwork
codes.definition: The administrative labor of securing emigration is borne by the mother, with the father abroad and the child too young to participate.
codes.model_rationale: The narrative consistently assigns the woman and mother as the on-the-ground executor of bureaucracy, revealing a gendered division of migration labor.

codes.label: Minor's subsumed legal status in family migration
codes.definition: As a child, the narrator did not independently pursue paperwork; their travel authorization was bundled into the mother's passport and approvals.
codes.model_rationale: The narrator's legal identity as a migrant is mediated entirely through the mother, illustrating how minors migrate as legal dependents rather than autonomous actors.

codes.label: Compulsory military service as constraint on emigration
codes.definition: Mandatory three-year army service bound brothers to the home country and shaped which family members could leave and in what order.
codes.model_rationale: The conscription system is presented as a structural force that determined individual family members' eligibility to emigrate.

codes.label: Family tragedy reshaping migration trajectory
codes.definition: A brother's accidental death on the eve of his own army call-up forced another brother to leave military service early to take over the farm, redirecting the family's migration plan.
codes.model_rationale: The narrative treats the death as a turning point that reorganized the family's responsibilities and migration sequencing.

codes.label: Selective and staged family emigration
codes.definition: The family could not move as a unit; only the mother and narrator left initially, with a brother planned to follow later after his own obligations were resolved.
codes.model_rationale: Emigration is depicted as a partial, sequenced process shaped by the constraints documented in adjacent codes, rather than a single collective departure.

codes.label: Child's present-orientation at moment of departure
codes.definition: The narrator did not anticipate missing friends or community, reflecting a developmental inability to project future loss at the point of leaving.
codes.model_rationale: The interviewer's prompt about missing friends is met with the child's admission of not having thought about it, suggesting a pre-reflective stance toward the costs of migration.

codes.label: Language attrition after emigration
codes.definition: School-acquired German faded after migration because no German-speaking community sustained it in the new environment.
codes.model_rationale: The narrator names the loss of a formally learned language as a direct consequence of relocating to a community without speakers.

codes.label: Retrospective awareness of migration's cultural costs
codes.definition: With distance, the narrator expresses regret at having lost the German language, indicating a later-in-life reckoning with what emigration cost culturally.
codes.model_rationale: The wistful 'it would be nice if I had remembered it' signals a retrospective evaluation of migration that contrasts with the untroubled child-perspective of the departure.

codes.label: Multi-stage transnational transit composition
codes.definition: The migration journey is assembled from successive legs—train, ocean liner, train, and bus—requiring transfers at hubs such as Chicago and a final railhead before local dispersal.
codes.model_rationale: Codes the staging concept of how the trip was constructed, not the individual itinerary steps; the legs are analytically linked as a single migrant transit strategy.

codes.label: Class-based accommodation discrepancy and peer advocacy
codes.definition: A first-class ticket did not secure a first-class berth; the family was relegated to a lower deck and only moved to second class after a previously-arrived fellow passenger recognized the ticket and intervened.
codes.model_rationale: Captures the gap between economic purchase and actual treatment, and the reliance on insiders who knew the ship's social and bureaucratic ordering.

codes.label: Institutional food provisioning as class-transcending constraint
codes.definition: Shipboard meals were repetitive and basic—fish and macaroni—continuing even after the upgrade, reflecting the limits of Italian maritime provisioning rather than ticket class.
codes.model_rationale: Reads food as a uniform institutional layer that overrides the class system implied by the ticket purchase.

codes.label: Immigrant medical inspection and exclusion regime
codes.definition: The voyage was interrupted by US-driven inquiries about overdue passengers; travelers with eye disease were detained and sent back; on arrival the family was vaccinated and medically inspected.
codes.model_rationale: Codes the public-health and quota-enforcement apparatus acting on the migrant body, treating the ship as a site of state screening.

codes.label: Encounter with American consumer novelty
codes.definition: The first sight of bananas on a US train functions as a sensory marker of arrival in American abundance.
codes.model_rationale: Treats the banana detail as an analytically loaded threshold event rather than a mere itinerary item, registering the new-world consumer landscape.

codes.label: Communal provisioning among co-traveling migrants
codes.definition: Mothers pooled purchased food to feed the children of a man who had provisioned little for his own kids, evidencing reciprocal in-transit care within the traveling party.
codes.model_rationale: Codes substitutive kinship and shared resources as a stabilizing mechanism of the migrant convoy, beyond the biological family.

codes.label: Co-migration of regional origin networks to a common US destination
codes.definition: Multiple families from the same European area traveled and transferred together across the ocean and the US interior, ultimately resettling in the same Utah town.
codes.model_rationale: Captures the chain-migration pattern by which a place-of-origin network reproduced itself as a place-of-destination cluster.

codes.label: Final-mile dispersal to a small-town mining settlement
codes.definition: After the transcontinental leg, the family completed the journey by bus from a railhead to a small mining town, converting an arrival into local resettlement.
codes.model_rationale: Codes the closing leg that resolves the long-haul transit into a specific rural-industrial destination, distinct from the staging concept that frames the whole journey.

codes.label: Company housing with co-ethnic clustering
codes.definition: Immigrant families housed in employer-owned dwellings alongside other households of similar national origin, enabling early ethnic networks.
codes.model_rationale: Names the industrial housing arrangement and the ethnic clustering it produced, an analytic feature of mining-town life.

codes.label: Navigating language barrier through non-verbal academic domains
codes.definition: Arithmetic and writing offer an academic foothold because they do not require spoken English.
codes.model_rationale: Identifies the specific curricular channel through which a non-English-speaking child could demonstrate competence.

codes.label: Retrospective self-distance from childhood bravado
codes.definition: Speaker reflects on their younger self with a measure of detachment, contrasting present disposition with past behavior.
codes.model_rationale: Marks the interpretive stance of the adult narrator looking back on the child who competed, a reflexive analytic move.

codes.label: Redefinition of female labor after emigration
codes.definition: Mother's shift from managing agricultural animals to domestic housework as the primary form of adjustment to American life.
codes.model_rationale: Codes the conceptual transition in women's labor roles, not a list of chores.

codes.label: War fear overriding repatriation desire
codes.definition: Mother's wish to return home was ultimately subordinated to fear that a second European war would endanger her.
codes.model_rationale: Surface-level longing masks an underlying prioritization of physical safety over emotional return.

codes.label: Transnational familial war trauma
codes.definition: Distant relatives directly harmed by wartime combat, extending the war's reach into the diaspora's emotional world.
codes.model_rationale: Codes the cross-border propagation of war consequences rather than specific biographical details of the brother.

codes.label: Progressive paternal health decline eroding breadwinner role
codes.definition: Father's deteriorating health limiting his capacity to work across successive job attempts until total incapacity.
codes.model_rationale: Names the analytic trajectory of declining earning capacity rather than logging each employment attempt.

codes.label: Generational divergence in diasporic longing
codes.definition: Mother's intense homesickness stands in contrast to the narrator's own comparatively muted emotional response.
codes.model_rationale: The unfinished admission signals an analytic distinction between first- and second-generation attachments to the homeland.

codes.label: Property transfer finalizing permanent emigration
codes.definition: Conveyance of the homeland estate to the brother marks an irrevocable severance from the country of origin.
codes.model_rationale: The act of giving up property symbolizes a deeper closing-off of the return option beyond the literal transaction.

codes.label: Arrival coinciding with economic crisis
codes.definition: The relocation to Colorado and marriage aligned temporally with the onset of severe Depression-era hardship.
codes.model_rationale: Codes the macro-economic timing that frames the family's subsequent struggles rather than the hardship itself.

codes.label: Rapid socioeconomic incorporation into co-ethnic community
codes.definition: Quick attainment of employment and spousal partnership shortly after arrival, facilitated by an established ethnic community.
codes.model_rationale: Finding jobs for both partners and meeting a future spouse between New Year's and May within months of arriving signals how the receiving community absorbed newcomers into its economic and social fabric.

codes.label: Endogamy within shared regional origin
codes.definition: Marriage between partners drawn from the same locality or region of origin, reinforcing existing migrant networks.
codes.model_rationale: The husband being 'from the same area' indexes marriage patterns that consolidated co-ethnic and co-regional ties rather than dispersing them through mixed unions.

codes.label: Settlement-cluster formation through chain migration
codes.definition: Ethnic enclaves grow as initial settlers attract and absorb successive newcomers, who prefer to integrate within the existing group.
codes.model_rationale: Grande's account of 'a settlement' where newcomers 'just like to integrate together' names the chain-migration mechanism that produced the Slovenian concentration around Denver.

codes.label: Latent preference for in-group clustering over dispersion
codes.definition: An underlying assumption that migrants actively choose co-ethnic proximity for comfort, support, or belonging rather than scattering across the host city.
codes.model_rationale: The phrasing 'they just like to integrate together' implies a subject-driven preference for in-group settlement, framing the enclave as a choice rather than a constraint.

codes.label: Persistent transnational connection through return visits
codes.definition: Sustained engagement with the place of origin via repeated return trips, even decades after emigration.
codes.model_rationale: Three return trips by car and plane, plus further visits, indicate that the homeland remained a live social geography rather than an abandoned past.

codes.label: Long-term settlement permanence
codes.definition: A stable, uninterrupted residence in the destination city extending from initial arrival through the rest of the life course.
codes.model_rationale: 'I've been here ever since' marks a one-way, permanent rooting in Denver that anchors the section's narrative arc.

codes.label: Economic precarity and household strain during the Depression
codes.definition: The speaker frames the Depression era as financially rough, marked by the husband's irregular income and the out-of-pocket burden of educating children in parochial school.
codes.model_rationale: These sentences together articulate the analytic concept of Depression-era household precarity, with school tuition costs as a salient pressure point — not merely a chronology of events.

codes.label: Long-tenure meatpacking labor as economic mainstay
codes.definition: Sustained, nearly three-decade employment in a packing house doing physically demanding meat-cutting work served as the household's economic anchor.
codes.model_rationale: The duration ('close to thirty years') and the specificity of the cutting/sausage work elevate this beyond a job mention to a concept of working-class industrial tenure.

codes.label: Intergenerational workplace co-participation in the plant
codes.definition: Mother and daughter worked in the same packing house, illustrating the transmission of working-class industrial labor across generations within immigrant families.
codes.model_rationale: Mitzi's entry into the same plant and progression from floor to smokehouse is coded as a concept of kin-based labor transmission rather than as a one-off itinerary step.

codes.label: Health-driven interruption and exit from industrial work
codes.definition: Physical and medical vulnerability repeatedly disrupts or terminates packing-house employment for both the speaker and her daughter.
codes.model_rationale: The recurring motif of surgeries and medical forced exit shows that bodily health, not choice, structured the arc of factory work — an analytic point about the body as a labor constraint.

codes.label: Naturalization and habitual civic participation
codes.definition: Acquiring U.S. citizenship in 1932 and voting in every subsequent election constitutes a practiced, ritualized form of civic belonging.
codes.model_rationale: The pairing of naturalization date with continuous voting encodes a concept of citizenship-as-practice rather than a mere biographical fact.

codes.label: Chosen American identity and affective belonging
codes.definition: The speaker's expressed happiness at returning from Europe and declaration that 'This is my country' reveal an emotionally invested, self-chosen national identification despite immigrant origins.
codes.model_rationale: The affective framing — joy at return, emphatic ownership ('This is my country') — points to an underlying claim of belonging that contrasts with her immigrant status, warranting a latent code.

sections.gist: Interview introduction covering the subject's age, original name Mary Floun, and birth date discrepancies between Christmas morning and official registration
Childhood recollections of the family home in London, including the Irish neighbor, the icebox, and the surrounding neighborhood
Mother Sophie Vivyeika's bakery on Commercial Road, her origins in Warsaw, and father Morris Floun's background in Poland and the army
Parents' marriage story, mother's first husband, conflict with the mother-in-law, and the family's move to London where they opened a bakery
Family composition of nine children, including the two sisters left in Poland with their grandmother and the order of siblings born in London
Father Morris's bakery work in London, the Russian army recall, his departure to New York a year ahead, and opening a downtown bakery
The ocean voyage on the ship Philadelphia, including the Jewish section, bunk beds in steerage, dancing for money, kosher food from the chef, and seeing the Statue of Liberty
Arrival in New York, reunion with relatives around the city, impressions of the Lower East Side with horse-drawn delivery wagons, and the family apartment at 141 Essex Street
Living conditions in the tenement walkup, shared bathrooms, gas meters, and the children's experiences starting school in America
Helping in the family bakery, meeting husband Jack through the bakery delivery work, having six children, and the family's move to Rockaway Beach after fifteen years on the East Side
Closing reflections on relatives gathering at the bakery, the parents' feelings about America, and present-day life with daughters, grandchildren, and the final sign-off

sentences.text: LEVINE:	Today—today is February the—the 12th and I’m here in Sunrise, Florida at the home of Minnie Rodwin.
Minnie came from England in 1910 when she was three and a half years of age.
RODWIN:	I—at that time I was Mary.
LEVINE:	You were Mary Floun .
Mary Floun, her—that was her maiden name.
RODWIN:	Yeah.
LEVINE:	And that was her name when she came to this country.
RODWIN:	Right.
LEVINE:	Okay.
And let’s see.
At the time of this—of this interview you’re 90 years of age.
You just had a birthday.
RODWIN:	Right.
LEVINE:	(chuckles)  Okay, so you’re 90 years old.
And this is Janet Levine for the National Park Service.
If you would state for the tape again your birth date and where you were born.
RODWIN:	My—the regular [unclear]?
LEVINE:	What you—whatever birth date.
You—you have two, right?
When you were born and when you were registered.
RODWIN:	December—December 25th.
LEVINE:	Was that when you were actually born?
RODWIN:	Yeah, six o’clock in the morning.
My—my—my—my mother used to say the—the times [unclear].
LEVINE:	Really?
And what else did your mother tell you about when you were born?
RODWIN:	Well, my mother was—business.
She’s—bakery business.
LEVINE:	In London?
RODWIN:	Yeah.
LEVINE:	Uh-huh, uh-huh.

So you were really born on Christmas—

RODWIN:	Morning.
LEVINE:	—morning.
And—but you were registered and so your official birth date is February 9th, 1907.
RODWIN:	That’s right.
LEVINE:	Okay.
Now, you were only three and a half when you came to this country.
But what recollections do you have of England?
RODWIN:	Well, first of all, we—we—we—we—we had—they were building—the building they had built—and they were building houses.
So there was a lady right—right—right opposite us.
She was Irish.
And she used to call me “Mary, Mary.”
So I said, “What do you want?”
“I just want to tell you not to make so—so—noise.”
I [unclear] I was quiet.
LEVINE:	Yeah, uh-huh.

RODWIN:	But—

LEVINE:	Do you remember the house you lived in in London?

RODWIN:	I have the birth—birth certif—

LEVINE:	Certificate.
Uh-huh, uh-huh.
But do you remember the house?
Do you remember anything in particular that you could describe?
RODWIN:	Well, I know we had—we had a—oh, what do you call it?
Where none—lo—lot of people didn’t have.
We had—outside, we had a—an icebox with ice.
And we used to keep our cold things in there.
LEVINE:	Uh-huh, uh-huh.
And a lot of people didn’t have an icebox at that time.
RODWIN:	No.
LEVINE:	Uh-huh, uh-huh.
And is there anything else you can think of that you recall?
Do you recall your mo—where your mother had the bakery?
RODWIN:	Yeah, in Commercial Road.
LEVINE:	Wow!
Uh-huh.
Did you go to the bakery?

Could you say anything about—

RODWIN:	Yeah.
We lived—we lived up where—where the bakery was.
LEVINE:	I see.
And what was your mother’s name?
RODWIN:	Sophie.
LEVINE:	Sophie.
And her maiden name, do you know that?
RODWIN:	It’s a funny name.
Vivyeika.
That means, oh, a squirrel.
LEVINE:	Oh.
(chuckles)  Uh-huh.
And could you spell it?

RODWIN:	Vivyeika—V-I-V-Y-E-I-K-A.

LEVINE:	Was your mother born in England?
RODWIN:	No.
LEVINE:	Where was she born?
RODWIN:	[unclear], Warsaw.
LEVINE:	Oh, Warsaw.
Uh-huh.
So do you know when she came to England?

Do you know—had she been—

RODWIN:	When we—when we were born.
LEVINE:	Well, I mean, had she been living—she had a bakery there so she must have been there for a while before you were born.
RODWIN:	No.
LEVINE:	No?

RODWIN:	When—er--my father was married and he was in—what do you call it’s—

LEVINE:	The military?
RODWIN:	No—yeah.
LEVINE:	In the—in the army.
RODWIN:	Yeah.
LEVINE:	Uh-huh.
RODWIN:	But he wasn’t [unclear].
He was in—see, I forget all this.

codes.label: Staged pioneer migration with father as advance party
codes.definition: A migration pattern in which one parent (here the father) departs a year ahead of the rest of the family to establish a foothold before bringing them over.
codes.model_rationale: The 'year before' phrasing and the sequence of father-then-family depicts a classic pioneer/preparatory migration strategy rather than a single joint departure.

codes.label: Migration timing tied to family formation
codes.definition: The mother’s arrival in England is positioned as coinciding with the birth of the children, suggesting migration was timed around family founding.
codes.model_rationale: Frames the move from Warsaw to the East End as part of establishing a household rather than as an isolated economic decision, even though the interviewer questions this sequencing.

codes.label: Kin-based reception network in destination country
codes.definition: Relatives already settled in New York provided the social infrastructure that made the father's relocation and subsequent family reunion feasible.
codes.model_rationale: The mention of relatives in New York in the same breath as obtaining tickets illustrates the chain-migration logic by which prior settlers enable newcomers.

codes.label: Pre-migration occupational continuity as economic foothold
codes.definition: Carrying a homeland trade into the new country provided an immediate basis for the family business and household stability.
codes.model_rationale: The confirmation that the mother was already a baker in Poland and the opening of the bakery on the Lower East Side illustrates the analytic idea of transferable skills underwriting immigrant economic establishment.

codes.label: Onomastic transformation across the life course
codes.definition: A change of personal name from the name borne in childhood or at emigration to the name carried in later life, marking biographical transitions such as immigration or marriage.
codes.model_rationale: The subject is introduced by her married/later name (Minnie Rodwin) but the interviewer explicitly recovers the originary name (Mary Floun) borne at the time of emigration, evidencing a recognized shift in personal name across the life course.

codes.label: Divergence between registered date and biographical date
codes.definition: Instances in which an officially recorded date for a life event differs from the date recalled or reported by the subject or family, registering a gap between bureaucratic documentation and lived/familial memory.
codes.model_rationale: The interviewer's explicit prompting that the subject 'has two' birth dates and the confirmation of a Christmas-morning birth against a February 9th registration marks a documented case of official-versus-biographical date divergence.

codes.label: Maternal oral tradition as biographical authority
codes.definition: The practice of treating a mother's remembered account as the primary source for details of one's own origins, including specifics absent from official records and supplying the only access to early-life information.
codes.model_rationale: The subject's knowledge of her precise birth hour ('six o'clock in the morning') and of her mother's bakery business in London is sourced specifically through the mother's verbal account, positioning maternal speech as the authoritative archive for unrecorded biography.

codes.label: Ritualized anchoring of subject in time, place, name, and institutional context
codes.definition: The conventional opening move of an oral history interview in which the interviewer fixes the subject's identity by eliciting current age, originary name, location, birth date, and the conducting institution.
codes.model_rationale: The interviewer's repeated checks of age, place, institutional affiliation (National Park Service), and request to restate biographical data for the tape exemplify the standardized anchoring ritual by which oral history sessions authenticate the subject and situate the testimony.

codes.label: Fragmented early childhood memory and the limits of recollection
codes.definition: The speaker's pre-verbal-era memories (from age three and a half) surface only as discrete fragments, requiring active probing by the interviewer to elicit any descriptive content at all.
codes.model_rationale: The repeated interviewer prompts and the speaker's halting, 'what do you call it?' retrieval show that very early memories are sparse, requiring scaffolding rather than flowing narrative.

codes.label: Interpersonal encounter as a vivid early memory anchor
codes.definition: A discrete, dialogically preserved episode with a neighbor functions as one of the few fully realized memory fragments from this earliest period, suggesting emotionally charged social interactions anchor recollection more than physical settings.
codes.model_rationale: The encounter is recalled in quoted dialogue ('Mary, Mary,' 'I just want to tell you not to make so—so—noise'), indicating it is preserved at a sensory/conversational level rather than as abstract description.

codes.label: Ethnic composition of the neighborhood as salient memory marker
codes.definition: The Irish identity of the neighbor across the street is singled out and preserved in the speaker's memory, indicating that ethnic/immigrant character of the surrounding community was a notable feature of the remembered landscape.
codes.model_rationale: The redundancy of naming the neighbor's nationality ('She was Irish') and its placement as a defining detail implies the speaker's surroundings were ethnically marked in ways that stood out for a memory operating with very few available details.

codes.label: Misrecognition and the construction of childhood identity
codes.definition: The neighbor addresses the child by a name that is not hers, revealing either unfamiliarity with the family or the child's liminal, not-fully-known status in the neighborhood, with implications for how identity is socially conferred in early childhood.
codes.model_rationale: Beyond a one-off name confusion, the misnaming signals the family's recent arrival or marginal social position in the street, framing the child as someone not yet known to the adult neighbors.

codes.label: Childhood self-defense against perceived unfair criticism
codes.definition: The speaker's preserved protest ('I was quiet') against the noise accusation shows that the memory is filtered through a residual sense of grievance, with the child remembered as defending her own conduct against adult reproach.
codes.model_rationale: The affective charge of the episode is preserved as a small injustice remembered, an analytic pattern in which early memories of authority figures often carry a self-justifying layer.

codes.label: Material culture as memory anchor and class marker
codes.definition: A specific domestic object (the icebox) operates simultaneously as a concrete retrieval cue for otherwise inaccessible early memory and as a symbol of the family's modest socioeconomic distinction from neighbors who lacked such an appliance.
codes.model_rationale: The interviewer and speaker collaboratively build the icebox into a shared marker, repeatedly contrasting it with what 'a lot of people didn't have,' showing how physical possessions do double duty as memory aid and class signifier.

codes.label: Tension between documentary record and embodied memory
codes.definition: The interviewer's invocation of the birth certificate as a known fact about the house, juxtaposed with the speaker's inability to describe that house, foregrounds the gap between official documentation and personal, embodied recollection.
codes.model_rationale: The exchange makes explicit that the speaker's factual address can be verified on paper while remaining experientially blank, a methodological and analytic tension central to oral history of pre-verbal or pre-migratory childhood.

codes.label: Co-location of family enterprise and household
codes.definition: The family lived at the same premises as the bakery on Commercial Road, blending domestic and commercial space.
codes.model_rationale: A common pattern among immigrant small-business households where home and shop coincide; the family did not merely work at the bakery but resided there.

codes.label: Eastern European origins of both parents
codes.definition: Both the mother and the father traced their origins to Poland, with the mother specifically from Warsaw, situating the family within a Polish-Jewish diaspora.
codes.model_rationale: Establishes the transnational migration background of the parental generation and hints at endogamous marriage patterns within a shared sending community.

codes.label: Folk etymology of family name as cultural marker
codes.definition: The mother’s maiden name Vivyeika is glossed by the interviewee as meaning 'squirrel,' preserving a piece of linguistic heritage.
codes.model_rationale: The interviewee flags the name as 'funny' and offers a vernacular translation, signalling that the unusual Eastern European surname carries cultural meaning for him.

codes.label: Paternal military service
codes.definition: The father’s army background is established as a salient biographical feature of his life before settling in England.
codes.model_rationale: Repeated back-and-forth identification of the father as a soldier makes this a key biographical anchor, even though specific details are not supplied.

codes.label: Memory gaps in biographical recall
codes.definition: The interviewee repeatedly signals inability to remember specific details of the family’s pre-migration life, particularly around origins and the father’s army service.
codes.model_rationale: Phrases like 'I forget all this' and 'I knew all these things but the—' reveal how late-life oral history is shaped by forgotten specifics, especially regarding the country of origin and the father’s military biography.

codes.label: Family assembly through transnational migration and remarriage
codes.definition: The parental household was constituted across borders and through sequential unions, including the mother's prior widowhood and remarriage to the father after his return.
codes.model_rationale: Captures how the parents' marriage was embedded in a chain of transnational movement and marital recomposition rather than treating the prior marriage or the return as isolated itinerary steps.

codes.label: Chronic in-law antagonism
codes.definition: Persistent harassment from the mother-in-law directed at the mother, disrupting the marital relationship.
codes.model_rationale: Names the ongoing interpersonal pattern rather than the single instance of conflict.

codes.label: Displaced grief as moral accusation
codes.definition: The mother-in-law reframes her son's death as caused by the mother's 'waiting,' transmuting bereavement into blame against the new wife.
codes.model_rationale: Goes beyond the surface antagonism to identify the underlying causal narrative in which grief is displaced onto the woman the deceased was waiting for.

codes.label: Geographic relocation as escape from family conflict
codes.definition: Moving to a new city as a strategy to remove the family from a hostile in-law's orbit.
codes.model_rationale: Frames the move to London as a coping strategy in response to harassment rather than as a neutral itinerary step.

codes.label: Immigrant self-employment via small trade
codes.definition: Opening a small family business (bakery) as the economic foundation of the reconstituted household in the new city.
codes.model_rationale: Abstracts the bakery opening into a broader analytic category of how refugee/immigrant households established livelihoods, rather than logging it as a one-off plot point.

codes.label: Disaggregated family migration across borders
codes.definition: The family did not relocate as a single unit; members were split between Poland and England and moved in stages, with parents preceding children and some children born in the host country.
codes.model_rationale: The analytic point is not 'nine children' or 'went to London' as itinerary events, but the pattern of selective, staggered migration that separated kin across national borders.

codes.label: Caregiving-capacity threshold as migration filter
codes.definition: Children were left behind because the mother judged she could not adequately care for them, framing who migrates as a function of perceived ability to provide for them.
codes.model_rationale: Rodwin attributes the separation to maternal fear of incapacity to care for the two sisters, revealing an implicit calculus whereby caregiving capacity, not preference, gates which children move.

codes.label: Grandmother as kin-keeper in country of origin
codes.definition: Extended kin (grandmother) assumed the parental caregiving role for children who remained in Poland, enabling the parents' departure for London.
codes.model_rationale: The grandmother functions structurally as the substitute parent for the left-behind children; her role is the mechanism that makes disaggregated migration feasible.

codes.label: Photographic mediation of absent kin
codes.definition: Familial relationships with members not co-present during early life are known and accessed through photographs rather than through embodied memory.
codes.model_rationale: Rodwin's claim that he 'just' knows his grandmother 'from pictures' signals that diaspora kinship is partially constituted by visual media standing in for lived contact.

codes.label: Birth-order self-location within the diaspora sibling cohort
codes.definition: Personal identity is anchored by position among siblings born in the host country rather than by a single global birth order within the whole family.
codes.model_rationale: 'Next to the youngest' locates the speaker within the England-born subset of the nine, showing that the post-migration cohort, not the whole sibling set, supplies the reference group for ordinal identity.

codes.label: Planned bounded separation between parents and left-behind children
codes.definition: The parting of the two sisters from their mother was framed as a temporary arrangement with a defined endpoint rather than as an open-ended rupture.
codes.model_rationale: 'Just till one was seven years old' encodes an intended horizon of reunion, presenting the separation as a managed interval rather than as definitive loss; the phrase's casual brevity contrasts with the gravity such separations could carry.

codes.label: Military conscription recall as emigration push factor
codes.definition: The father's recall to the Russian Army functioned as the precipitating threat that made the family choose to leave Europe for the United States.
codes.model_rationale: The narrator and interviewer explicitly identify the army recall as the reason for departure, establishing conscription as a causal driver rather than mere background context.

codes.label: Family roster that preserves the memory of a deceased sibling
codes.definition: The enumeration of children includes Yettie, who died, showing that oral family histories integrate lost members into the remembered order of births.
codes.model_rationale: Yettie's inclusion in the birth order and the narrator's terse announcement of her death mark how family reckonings hold space for the deceased as a structural part of the sibling list.

codes.label: Confused postwar military obligations across shifting borders
codes.definition: The 'underground' Russian Army to which the father was recalled reflects how redrawn East European borders left civilian populations subject to ambiguous conscription claims from successor states.
codes.model_rationale: The interviewer's prompting ('Maybe it was the Russian Army,' 'those borders changed a lot') and the narrator's 'underground' qualifier expose the geopolitical indeterminacy underlying the recall, a latent condition of interwar Jewish migration.

codes.label: Ethnic/religious segregation as organizing principle in transit
