codes.definition: Ships and other transit infrastructures assigned passengers to designated sections by ethnicity or religion, here a Jewish section, functioning as a form of diaspora community continuity at sea.
codes.model_rationale: The interviewer's surprise at the existence of a Jewish section suggests it was a structural feature of steerage travel that institutionalized communal clustering.

codes.label: Steerage as cramped, shared, low-status accommodation
codes.definition: Passengers in the lowest deck ('bottom of the ship') shared crowded quarters with bunk beds, marking class-based spatial segregation.
codes.model_rationale: Describes the material conditions of third-class travel and the shared, dense living arrangement.

codes.label: Children performing for tips among passengers
codes.definition: Young passengers entertained fellow travelers through dancing and singing, receiving thrown coins as informal compensation in a liminal, unregulated economy.
codes.model_rationale: Captures the informal child-labor/economy that arose in steerage spaces of entertainment and tipping.

codes.label: Maternal moral gatekeeping of children's earnings
codes.definition: The mother acted as a normative authority figure, restricting children's access to money earned through informal performance to uphold propriety or dignity.
codes.model_rationale: Beyond literal refusal, this signals a parental stance on class respectability and protecting children from informal street-economy norms.

codes.label: Diaspora networks reproducing kosher practice at sea
codes.definition: Co-religionist crew members (here a chef) leveraged prior acquaintance and shared observance to smuggle or provision kosher food to passengers, sustaining religious dietary law outside the home.
codes.model_rationale: Shows how Jewish networks operated institutionally and informally across borders to maintain religious practice in transit.

codes.label: Iconic arrival imagery as memory anchor
codes.definition: The Statue of Liberty sighting functions as a vivid, enduring collective memory marker of arrival in America, often recalled alongside other sensory impressions.
codes.model_rationale: The rush to see the Statue of Liberty is a canonical immigrant-arrival motif anchoring episodic memory of the journey's endpoint.

codes.label: Child vulnerability and informal caregiving in transit
codes.definition: A fellow passenger intervened when the child soiled himself, undressing, cleaning, and redressing him, illustrating how parenting and caregiving roles were diffused among strangers during travel.
codes.model_rationale: Highlights the precariousness of child supervision in steerage and the communal caregiving that filled gaps left by overstretched parents.

codes.label: Cross-national linguistic identity in parent-child bond
codes.definition: Persistence of a British/English term of address for the father ('Daddy') over the Yiddish-influenced 'Papa,' signaling the formative influence of the London transit stop on the child's identity.
codes.model_rationale: The narrator's own explanation that 'in London it was Daddy' frames the term of address as a marker of the prior national context, an analytic point about layered national identity rather than a simple vocabulary item.

codes.label: Wife as established tradesperson in immigrant economy
codes.definition: The wife brought a craft (baking) from the homeland, and the husband shifted into her trade, inverting the typical expectation of male occupational continuity.
codes.model_rationale: The exchange in which the husband, a tailor in Poland, becomes a baker 'from your mother' encodes a gendered pattern of skill transfer within the family enterprise, not just a biographical fact.

codes.label: Childhood sensory memory of pre-automotive urban landscape
codes.definition: The horse-drawn delivery wagons register as a vivid first impression of the Lower East Side, marking the city as still partially animal-powered in the child's experience.
codes.model_rationale: The wagons are recalled not as itinerary but as a salient sensory marker of a transitional urban era, an analytic point about how childhood memory preserves the texture of a city before mechanization.

codes.label: Tenement housing pattern on the Lower East Side
codes.definition: Residence in a four-story walkup tenement at a specific LES address, typical of early-twentieth-century immigrant settlement.
codes.model_rationale: The precise address, walkup configuration, and identification of the building as a tenement point to a recurring residential form of immigrant family life, not merely a personal address.

codes.label: Selective childhood memory of the migration journey
codes.definition: The child retains vivid images of the destination neighborhood but no memory of the Ellis Island processing, illustrating how early trauma or bureaucratic moments are filtered out of recollection.
codes.model_rationale: The contrast between complete amnesia for Ellis Island and detailed sensory recall of the LES suggests an analytic pattern in which arrival experience is selectively preserved, with the institutional processing point elided in favor of neighborhood impressions.

codes.label: Communal and pay-per-use tenement infrastructure
codes.definition: Basic building facilities (toilets, gas) were either shared between apartments or required coin insertion per use, marking the material conditions of recent-arrival housing on the Lower East Side.
codes.model_rationale: Both the hall-shared bathroom and quarter-fed gas meters are described as routine features of the Essex Street apartment, signaling a housing stock organized around collective or metered access rather than private provision.

codes.label: Institutional sanctioning of children's domestic labor over schooling
codes.definition: School authorities told the immigrant mother to keep her older children home to help her, revealing an institutional tolerance of child contribution to household survival that overrode routine expectations of school enrollment.
codes.model_rationale: The quoted directive ('Let them stay home and help you') from school personnel frames children's domestic labor as an acceptable alternative to attendance, illustrating how gatekeepers mediated immigrant children's access to formal education.

codes.label: Prior educational socialization in country of origin as a resettlement resource
codes.definition: The speaker's earlier school attendance from age three in London provided a foundational familiarity with classroom routines and knowledge that preceded and eased her entry into American schooling.
codes.model_rationale: The interviewer's repeated underscoring that the speaker 'already knew a lot of things' foregrounds pre-migration schooling as a portable asset brought into the resettlement experience, distinct from the brothers' situation.

codes.label: Neighborhood amenity proximity as texture of immigrant daily life
codes.definition: Walkable, immediately accessible local businesses such as the bakery across the street, paired with a positive affective recollection ('we had a nice time'), situate daily routines within a dense, service-rich immigrant neighborhood.
codes.model_rationale: The bakery's proximity and the speaker's warm summary gesture toward the everyday comforts of the block, capturing how proximate amenities anchored everyday life on the Lower East Side.

codes.label: Intergenerational family labor in ethnic enterprise
codes.definition: Children contributing labor to a family-owned business, especially around culturally specific or seasonal tasks.
codes.model_rationale: Rodwin describes helping her parents in the bakery, with holiday-specific work of decorating macaroons with almonds and cherries, showing child participation in a family trade tied to cultural foodways.

codes.label: Spousal meeting through shared trade
codes.definition: Romantic pairing originating in or through the same occupational sector or workplace.
codes.model_rationale: Husband was a bakery delivery driver, illustrating how the bakery industry itself connected the couple.

codes.label: Reflexive valuation of family cohesion
codes.definition: Retrospective framing of family unity as a defining and valued feature of life.
codes.model_rationale: When prompted to look back, Rodwin immediately names 'always together' as the salient quality, signaling family solidarity as a central, nostalgic theme rather than a stated plot point.

codes.label: Large-family childbearing pattern
codes.definition: Bearing many children, characteristic of early-twentieth-century immigrant working-class households.
codes.model_rationale: Six children indexes the demographic/family-formation norm of her cohort and class.

codes.label: Continuity of family enterprise across residential transitions
codes.definition: Carrying the family business forward when relocating rather than abandoning the trade.
codes.model_rationale: The family operated bakeries in both the Lower East Side and Rockaway Beach, treating the firm as a portable anchor of household life.

codes.label: Intra-urban relocation from immigrant enclave after long residence
codes.definition: Departure from a dense immigrant neighborhood to another part of the metropolitan area after an extended period of settlement.
codes.model_rationale: Roughly fifteen years on the East Side followed by a move to Rockaway Beach maps onto the classic immigrant trajectory of leaving the initial enclave for a more suburban destination.

codes.label: Fusion of family name and business identity
codes.definition: The family surname functioning simultaneously as the firm's name, indexing the inseparability of household and enterprise in the immigrant economy.
codes.model_rationale: The bakery is 'Floun's'—the maiden name—revealing that the kin unit and the firm were effectively one institution.

codes.label: Bakery as immigrant kin gathering site
codes.definition: The family business functioned as a recurring meeting point for relatives, illustrating how immigrant-owned enterprises served as anchors of kin networks within ethnic enclaves.
codes.model_rationale: Multiple sentences describe relatives regularly coming to the father's bakery on Fridays and gathering at the home, indicating the business doubled as a hub for kin ties rather than purely a commercial site.

codes.label: Spousal-contingent female migration
codes.definition: A pattern in which a woman's migration was conditional on her husband's decision, reflecting couple-based migration norms in which female mobility followed male partners.
codes.model_rationale: The mother is reported to have wanted to come to America only because the father was going, surfacing the dependent framing of female migration in this household.

codes.label: Positive retrospective evaluation of migration
codes.definition: A satisfied or favorable after-the-fact assessment of the migration experience and its outcomes for the immigrant generation.
codes.model_rationale: Both the narrator's own summary ('it was nice') and the reported contentment of the parents convey an unambiguously favorable retrospective stance toward the migration.

codes.label: Second-generation geographic dispersal
codes.definition: The scattering of adult immigrant-descendant children across distant regions of the host country, characteristic of post-migration family trajectories.
codes.model_rationale: Daughters settled near L.A. and in Atlanta, exemplifying the regional dispersion of the second generation away from the original immigrant enclave.

codes.label: Fading autobiographical memory in late life
codes.definition: Uncertainty or gaps in recall of formative life events associated with advanced age.
codes.model_rationale: The narrator's inability to confirm whether she visited Ellis Island is read as a marker of autobiographical memory limits at ninety years old, rather than as biographical fact.

codes.label: Understated contentment as evaluative register
codes.definition: A recurring tone of modest, undramatic satisfaction that characterizes the speaker's evaluations across multiple life domains and across time.
codes.model_rationale: Repeated uses of 'nice,' 'all right,' and 'they were all right' across past relatives, the migration experience, and present life suggest a stable affective register of low-key contentment rather than enthusiastic endorsement.

codes.label: Generational continuity through descendants
codes.definition: The presence of grandchildren as material evidence of an ongoing family lineage extending beyond the immigrant generation.
codes.model_rationale: The mention and offer to display photographs of grandchildren mark the third generation as a tangible continuation of the lineage established by the immigrant parents.

C0001.label: Kin-based resettlement infrastructure
C0001.definition: Reliance on existing family members (mother's sister) as an initial foothold and staging point in the new region.
C0001.model_rationale: Captures the social architecture of chain migration rather than the literal itinerary.

C0002.label: Selective and staged family emigration
C0002.definition: The family could not move as a unit; only the mother and narrator left initially, with a brother planned to follow later after his own obligations were resolved.
C0002.model_rationale: Emigration is depicted as a partial, sequenced process shaped by the constraints documented in adjacent codes, rather than a single collective departure.

C0003.label: Place of origin anchored to shifting imperial borders
C0003.definition: The speaker locates her village within a political geography that has changed (Austria-Hungary then Yugoslavia), tying personal identity to the dissolution of the prewar empire.
C0003.model_rationale: The speaker must gloss her village by reference to successive political formations, indicating that origin is narrated through geopolitical rupture rather than a stable homeland.

C0004.label: Material precarity and theft during transit
C0004.definition: Travelers arrived at a port with diminished belongings, having lost part of their clothing, leaving them without essentials for the remainder of the voyage.
C0004.model_rationale: The theft of clothes is presented as a concrete vulnerability that required outside remedy, marking the transit as materially insecure.

C0005.label: Transnational family coordination across borders
C0005.definition: Emigration is organized as a distributed effort between an absent father in the destination country and a mother handling logistics in the origin country, with letters and prepaid tickets initiating the process.
C0005.model_rationale: The section repeatedly frames the move as a multi-country project: father in America sending tickets and information, mother on the ground executing the steps.

C0006.label: Anticipatory joy centered on paternal reunion
C0006.definition: The child's emotional orientation toward emigration is dominated by eagerness to meet the father, eclipsing awareness of what is being left behind.
C0006.model_rationale: Several sentences express the ten-year-old's single-minded focus on reunification, framing emigration through longing for the father.

C0007.label: Paternal itinerant labor in extractive industries
C0007.definition: Father's pattern of moving among mining sites across states (Aspen, Tooele, Sunnyside) tied to coal-mine employment.
C0007.model_rationale: Captures the cross-state trajectory of the father's mining work as an analytic pattern rather than a travel itinerary.

C0008.label: Relocation triggered by paternal health decline
C0008.definition: Mobility decisions are forced by the working-age father's deteriorating health, ending the mining-town chapter.
C0008.model_rationale: Identifies bodily breakdown of the breadwinner as the structural cause of the next move, not preference.

C0009.label: Peer-mediated English acquisition
C0009.definition: Initial English vocabulary is picked up informally from co-ethnic neighbor children rather than taught in school.
C0009.model_rationale: Codes the social mechanism by which language was acquired, treating peer children as linguistic intermediaries.

C0010.label: Compressed school entry and accelerated grade placement
C0010.definition: Rapid enrollment after arrival and placement above the expected grade level, assuming equivalence with US peers.
C0010.model_rationale: Surfaces the school's institutional logic of rapid assimilation and age-based grade assignment despite limited English.

C0011.label: Staged, multi-port post-war transatlantic passage
C0011.definition: The voyage was not direct but involved an intermediate land stay and a ship that called at multiple ports, characteristic of disrupted early-1920s shipping.
C0011.model_rationale: The ship stopping 'in a lot of places' (London, Paris, etc.) after World War One is named by the speaker as a feature of the journey, distinguishing this transit from later direct steerage passages.

C0012.label: Reliance on a returning diaspora acquaintance for rescue
C0012.definition: Mother and daughter depended on a man known to the mother — apparently a returnee from America — to intervene and restore what had been lost.
C0012.model_rationale: The narrator frames recovery as contingent on a pre-existing personal connection to someone who had crossed the Atlantic in the opposite direction, illustrating how diasporic networks functioned during the journey itself.

C0013.label: American English fluency as diasporic capital
C0013.definition: The helpful acquaintance's value is explicitly located in his ability to 'speak American,' a competence tied to prior American residence that positioned him as an intermediary.
C0013.model_rationale: Highlighting his command of 'American' rather than his nationality or kinship treats linguistic fluency as the operative resource that enabled rescue in a foreign port.

C0014.label: Female kin unit navigating migration with constrained agency
C0014.definition: A mother-and-daughter pair undertakes the voyage together; their capacity to act in transit depends on the initiative of a male acquaintance, suggesting gendered structures of early-twentieth-century female migration.
C0014.model_rationale: The narrative centers a female traveling unit whose recovery from theft is performed by a man they happened to know, implicitly framing women's transatlantic mobility as mediated through male diasporic figures.

C0015.label: Transnational paternal absence and mother-headed farm
C0015.definition: The father's long-standing presence in the United States leaves the mother as the de facto operator of the rural household and farm, structuring the home the speaker describes.
C0015.model_rationale: The father is introduced as already in the US since 1911, while the mother is identified as the farmer who raises and barters cattle; this reframes the 'home' as a mother-run unit and foreshadows the migration narrative.

C0016.label: Integrated human-livestock dwelling as regional norm
C0016.definition: The peasant house and barn are built as a single structure with animals immediately adjacent to domestic rooms, a layout the speaker describes as historically typical of the community.
C0016.model_rationale: Multiple sentences describe the single-building layout, the attached byre and manure pile, and the speaker's insistence that 'the majority of people had it similar' and that it was a long-standing, inherited convention rather than an individual choice.

C0017.label: Children's emotional proximity to farm animals
C0017.definition: Livestock are framed not as utility objects but as everyday companions of childhood, with naming, affection, and the animals figured as part of 'regular home life'.
C0017.model_rationale: The speaker describes a beloved pet cow, names for the pigs, and the animals as 'part of my life' and 'just a regular home life,' indicating an affective, relational orientation toward livestock.

C0018.label: Seasonal slaughter and cured-meat provisioning cycle
C0018.definition: Pigs are killed in winter, the meat is salt-cured, weighted, and smoked to provide preserved food through the following summer, embedding the household in an annual preservation calendar.
C0018.model_rationale: The speaker links winter slaughter, the salt/water/rock cure, smoking, and the allocation of different cuts to different occasions, illustrating a deliberate temporal logic of food storage.

C0019.label: Children's obligatory participation in slaughter and butchery
C0019.definition: Children are expected to perform necessary but unpleasant tasks during animal slaughter — such as catching blood for blood sausage — as a normal part of their household labor.
C0019.model_rationale: Repeated 'I had to' formulations and the phrase 'part of my job' mark these tasks as required subsistence labor transmitted across generations, even when the child dislikes them.

C0020.label: Diversified mixed-livestock subsistence economy
C0020.definition: The household draws food and exchange value from a portfolio of animals — cows for milk, cheese, and barter; pigs for fresh and preserved meat; chickens for eggs and meat — rather than reliance on a single commodity.
C0020.model_rationale: Across the section the speaker enumerates cattle, pigs, and chickens each supplying different products, and notes beef is rare at home, capturing a multi-species household economy.

C0021.label: Mother as farm operator and processor of food
C0021.definition: The mother is the central agent who manages the farm, arranges hired labor for slaughter, and oversees the chain of preservation and cooking.
C0021.model_rationale: Sentences attribute farming, bartering, and the coordination of slaughter and sausage-making to the mother, marking her as the operative head of the rural enterprise.

C0022.label: Affective insider framing of farm life against outsider's utilitarian reading
C0022.definition: While the speaker describes the home-and-barn arrangement as ordinary and beloved, the interviewer reframes it instrumentally as 'getting your money's worth'; the speaker's emphatic 'Oh, yes' resists that commodifying lens.
C0022.model_rationale: The exchange between interviewer ('money's worth') and speaker (repetition of 'Oh, yes') captures a tension between an outside economistic view of peasant life and the speaker's embedded, affective belonging to it.

C0023.label: State-mandated domestic hospitality for refugees
C0023.definition: Government policy required civilian households to surrender private space (e.g., summer kitchens) to host refugees, translating state refugee policy into coerced household sharing.
C0023.model_rationale: Captures the analytic point that refugee absorption was state-imposed rather than voluntary, with domestic interiors converted into reception space.

C0024.label: Cross-language accommodation with displaced populations
C0024.definition: Hosts and refugees negotiated mutual intelligibility despite speaking different languages, managing communication barriers through pragmatic rather than fluent contact.
C0024.model_rationale: Highlights the everyday linguistic work required when linguistically distinct populations were forced into shared domestic space.

C0025.label: Riverine domestic labor under wartime conditions
C0025.definition: Women's routine household work (laundry) required travel to a distant river and washing on rocks, reflecting gendered division of domestic labor in a village without in-home facilities.
C0025.model_rationale: Names the analytic concept of river-based laundering as gendered domestic infrastructure rather than logging the literal trip as an event.

C0026.label: Environmental contamination from medical waste into shared water
C0026.definition: Hospital effluent and waste entered the same river used for civilian laundry, creating a public-health hazard tied to the spatial co-location of care and domestic labor sites.
C0026.model_rationale: Captures the structural overlap of medical and domestic water use and the resulting sanitary risk, including the mother's avoidance of the hospital due to its bloody environment.

C0027.label: Strategic risk navigation in everyday practice
C0027.definition: Households adapted routines to mitigate environmental hazards, here by deliberately washing upstream of the hospital discharge.
C0027.model_rationale: Reads the upstream-washing practice as tacit risk literacy, an analytic concept about how civilians internalize and act on infrastructural hazards.

C0028.label: Gender-segregated medical infrastructure
C0028.definition: Hospitals were physically and organizationally separated by sex, with men's and women's facilities on opposite sides of the river rather than co-located.
C0028.model_rationale: Documents the historical organization of care by gender and the speaker's awareness of this as a contrast to later consolidated arrangements.

C0029.label: Imperial ruling class visible in the local landscape
C0029.definition: Austrian elites (described as well-to-do rather than governmental) occupied prominent local structures such as the castle situated directly across from the hospital.
C0029.model_rationale: Captures the spatial proximity of imperial elite residence to civilian and medical infrastructure as an analytic feature of Habsburg-era village life.

C0030.label: Imperial linguistic hierarchy
C0030.definition: Slovenian functioned as the domestic language while German was the imposed administrative/official language required for navigating offices and public institutions.
C0030.model_rationale: Reads the bilingual situation as a hierarchy of imperial domination rather than neutral multilingualism, with German as the language of institutional access.

C0031.label: Erosion of prewar architectural and institutional landscape
C0031.definition: Prewar landmarks such as castles were repurposed into modern institutions (e.g., tuberculosis sanitarium), marking the disappearance of older structures under modernization.
C0031.model_rationale: Frames the speaker's lament about vanishing castles as an analytic observation about the transformation of the built environment from imperial to modern use.

C0032.label: Childhood peripheral awareness of regime change
C0032.definition: Major geopolitical transitions (Austria-Hungary to Yugoslavia, c. 1918–1920) are recalled only vaguely and from a child's vantage, with the Sarajevo assassination framing the war's origins in now-Yugoslav territory.
C0032.model_rationale: The speaker signals distance from formal politics through hedging ('all I kind of remember'), yet links the regime change to the Sarajevo trigger, showing how macro-history is absorbed by children as atmosphere rather than analysis.

C0033.label: Resigned civilian compliance under war conditions
C0033.definition: Ordinary people endure wartime with muted stoicism — excitement mixed with the recognition that one 'just had to go along' rather than resist.
C0033.model_rationale: The phrase 'you just had to go along' captures a civilian posture of pragmatic acquiescence to forces beyond individual control, distinct from fear or enthusiasm.

C0034.label: Wartime consumer restrictions
C0034.definition: Formal wartime prohibitions on purchasing certain goods, signaling state-imposed scarcity.
C0034.model_rationale: Cited as a defining feature of the war economy that pushed households toward non-market exchange.

C0035.label: Rural-urban barter complementarity under scarcity
C0035.definition: With formal markets constrained, countryside households (food surplus) exchange with city households (clothing surplus) across the river, sustaining both communities.
C0035.model_rationale: The exchange of eggs/chicken/milk for clothing illustrates a structural complementarity: rural and urban households held inverted surpluses, making barter a rational adaptation to wartime scarcity.

C0036.label: Pre-automotive river-crossing infrastructure
C0036.definition: Trans-local mobility depended on a bridge and horse-drawn wagons, a transport regime the speaker marks as superseded by automobiles.
C0036.model_rationale: The contrast between wagons (then) and cars (now) frames the village's link to the city as part of a vanishing pre-modern infrastructure, anchoring the barter system in physical proximity across the river.

C0037.label: Bureaucratic navigation of emigration approvals
C0037.definition: The emigration process required the mother to make repeated visits to multiple courts and offices to secure approvals from various authorities, intensified in the final month before departure.
C0037.model_rationale: Multiple sentences detail the volume and variety of administrative touchpoints the mother had to clear, characterizing the move as procedurally demanding.

C0038.label: Gendered burden of emigration paperwork
C0038.definition: The administrative labor of securing emigration is borne by the mother, with the father abroad and the child too young to participate.
C0038.model_rationale: The narrative consistently assigns the woman and mother as the on-the-ground executor of bureaucracy, revealing a gendered division of migration labor.

C0039.label: Minor's subsumed legal status in family migration
C0039.definition: As a child, the narrator did not independently pursue paperwork; their travel authorization was bundled into the mother's passport and approvals.
C0039.model_rationale: The narrator's legal identity as a migrant is mediated entirely through the mother, illustrating how minors migrate as legal dependents rather than autonomous actors.

C0040.label: Compulsory military service as constraint on emigration
C0040.definition: Mandatory three-year army service bound brothers to the home country and shaped which family members could leave and in what order.
C0040.model_rationale: The conscription system is presented as a structural force that determined individual family members' eligibility to emigrate.

C0041.label: Family tragedy reshaping migration trajectory
C0041.definition: A brother's accidental death on the eve of his own army call-up forced another brother to leave military service early to take over the farm, redirecting the family's migration plan.
C0041.model_rationale: The narrative treats the death as a turning point that reorganized the family's responsibilities and migration sequencing.

C0042.label: Child's present-orientation at moment of departure
C0042.definition: The narrator did not anticipate missing friends or community, reflecting a developmental inability to project future loss at the point of leaving.
C0042.model_rationale: The interviewer's prompt about missing friends is met with the child's admission of not having thought about it, suggesting a pre-reflective stance toward the costs of migration.

C0043.label: Language attrition after emigration
C0043.definition: School-acquired German faded after migration because no German-speaking community sustained it in the new environment.
C0043.model_rationale: The narrator names the loss of a formally learned language as a direct consequence of relocating to a community without speakers.

C0044.label: Retrospective awareness of migration's cultural costs
C0044.definition: With distance, the narrator expresses regret at having lost the German language, indicating a later-in-life reckoning with what emigration cost culturally.
C0044.model_rationale: The wistful 'it would be nice if I had remembered it' signals a retrospective evaluation of migration that contrasts with the untroubled child-perspective of the departure.

C0045.label: Multi-stage transnational transit composition
C0045.definition: The migration journey is assembled from successive legs—train, ocean liner, train, and bus—requiring transfers at hubs such as Chicago and a final railhead before local dispersal.
C0045.model_rationale: Codes the staging concept of how the trip was constructed, not the individual itinerary steps; the legs are analytically linked as a single migrant transit strategy.

C0046.label: Class-based accommodation discrepancy and peer advocacy
C0046.definition: A first-class ticket did not secure a first-class berth; the family was relegated to a lower deck and only moved to second class after a previously-arrived fellow passenger recognized the ticket and intervened.
C0046.model_rationale: Captures the gap between economic purchase and actual treatment, and the reliance on insiders who knew the ship's social and bureaucratic ordering.

C0047.label: Institutional food provisioning as class-transcending constraint
C0047.definition: Shipboard meals were repetitive and basic—fish and macaroni—continuing even after the upgrade, reflecting the limits of Italian maritime provisioning rather than ticket class.
C0047.model_rationale: Reads food as a uniform institutional layer that overrides the class system implied by the ticket purchase.

C0048.label: Immigrant medical inspection and exclusion regime
C0048.definition: The voyage was interrupted by US-driven inquiries about overdue passengers; travelers with eye disease were detained and sent back; on arrival the family was vaccinated and medically inspected.
C0048.model_rationale: Codes the public-health and quota-enforcement apparatus acting on the migrant body, treating the ship as a site of state screening.

C0049.label: Encounter with American consumer novelty
C0049.definition: The first sight of bananas on a US train functions as a sensory marker of arrival in American abundance.
C0049.model_rationale: Treats the banana detail as an analytically loaded threshold event rather than a mere itinerary item, registering the new-world consumer landscape.

C0050.label: Communal provisioning among co-traveling migrants
C0050.definition: Mothers pooled purchased food to feed the children of a man who had provisioned little for his own kids, evidencing reciprocal in-transit care within the traveling party.
C0050.model_rationale: Codes substitutive kinship and shared resources as a stabilizing mechanism of the migrant convoy, beyond the biological family.

C0051.label: Co-migration of regional origin networks to a common US destination
C0051.definition: Multiple families from the same European area traveled and transferred together across the ocean and the US interior, ultimately resettling in the same Utah town.
C0051.model_rationale: Captures the chain-migration pattern by which a place-of-origin network reproduced itself as a place-of-destination cluster.

C0052.label: Final-mile dispersal to a small-town mining settlement
C0052.definition: After the transcontinental leg, the family completed the journey by bus from a railhead to a small mining town, converting an arrival into local resettlement.
C0052.model_rationale: Codes the closing leg that resolves the long-haul transit into a specific rural-industrial destination, distinct from the staging concept that frames the whole journey.

C0053.label: Company housing with co-ethnic clustering
C0053.definition: Immigrant families housed in employer-owned dwellings alongside other households of similar national origin, enabling early ethnic networks.
C0053.model_rationale: Names the industrial housing arrangement and the ethnic clustering it produced, an analytic feature of mining-town life.

C0054.label: Navigating language barrier through non-verbal academic domains
C0054.definition: Arithmetic and writing offer an academic foothold because they do not require spoken English.
C0054.model_rationale: Identifies the specific curricular channel through which a non-English-speaking child could demonstrate competence.

C0055.label: Retrospective self-distance from childhood bravado
C0055.definition: Speaker reflects on their younger self with a measure of detachment, contrasting present disposition with past behavior.
C0055.model_rationale: Marks the interpretive stance of the adult narrator looking back on the child who competed, a reflexive analytic move.

C0056.label: Redefinition of female labor after emigration
C0056.definition: Mother's shift from managing agricultural animals to domestic housework as the primary form of adjustment to American life.
C0056.model_rationale: Codes the conceptual transition in women's labor roles, not a list of chores.

C0057.label: War fear overriding repatriation desire
C0057.definition: Mother's wish to return home was ultimately subordinated to fear that a second European war would endanger her.
C0057.model_rationale: Surface-level longing masks an underlying prioritization of physical safety over emotional return.

C0058.label: Transnational familial war trauma
C0058.definition: Distant relatives directly harmed by wartime combat, extending the war's reach into the diaspora's emotional world.
C0058.model_rationale: Codes the cross-border propagation of war consequences rather than specific biographical details of the brother.

C0059.label: Progressive paternal health decline eroding breadwinner role
C0059.definition: Father's deteriorating health limiting his capacity to work across successive job attempts until total incapacity.
C0059.model_rationale: Names the analytic trajectory of declining earning capacity rather than logging each employment attempt.

C0060.label: Generational divergence in diasporic longing
C0060.definition: Mother's intense homesickness stands in contrast to the narrator's own comparatively muted emotional response.
C0060.model_rationale: The unfinished admission signals an analytic distinction between first- and second-generation attachments to the homeland.

C0061.label: Property transfer finalizing permanent emigration
C0061.definition: Conveyance of the homeland estate to the brother marks an irrevocable severance from the country of origin.
C0061.model_rationale: The act of giving up property symbolizes a deeper closing-off of the return option beyond the literal transaction.

C0062.label: Arrival coinciding with economic crisis
C0062.definition: The relocation to Colorado and marriage aligned temporally with the onset of severe Depression-era hardship.
C0062.model_rationale: Codes the macro-economic timing that frames the family's subsequent struggles rather than the hardship itself.

C0063.label: Rapid socioeconomic incorporation into co-ethnic community
C0063.definition: Quick attainment of employment and spousal partnership shortly after arrival, facilitated by an established ethnic community.
C0063.model_rationale: Finding jobs for both partners and meeting a future spouse between New Year's and May within months of arriving signals how the receiving community absorbed newcomers into its economic and social fabric.

C0064.label: Endogamy within shared regional origin
C0064.definition: Marriage between partners drawn from the same locality or region of origin, reinforcing existing migrant networks.
C0064.model_rationale: The husband being 'from the same area' indexes marriage patterns that consolidated co-ethnic and co-regional ties rather than dispersing them through mixed unions.

C0065.label: Settlement-cluster formation through chain migration
C0065.definition: Ethnic enclaves grow as initial settlers attract and absorb successive newcomers, who prefer to integrate within the existing group.
C0065.model_rationale: Grande's account of 'a settlement' where newcomers 'just like to integrate together' names the chain-migration mechanism that produced the Slovenian concentration around Denver.

C0066.label: Latent preference for in-group clustering over dispersion
C0066.definition: An underlying assumption that migrants actively choose co-ethnic proximity for comfort, support, or belonging rather than scattering across the host city.
C0066.model_rationale: The phrasing 'they just like to integrate together' implies a subject-driven preference for in-group settlement, framing the enclave as a choice rather than a constraint.

C0067.label: Persistent transnational connection through return visits
C0067.definition: Sustained engagement with the place of origin via repeated return trips, even decades after emigration.
C0067.model_rationale: Three return trips by car and plane, plus further visits, indicate that the homeland remained a live social geography rather than an abandoned past.

C0068.label: Long-term settlement permanence
C0068.definition: A stable, uninterrupted residence in the destination city extending from initial arrival through the rest of the life course.
C0068.model_rationale: 'I've been here ever since' marks a one-way, permanent rooting in Denver that anchors the section's narrative arc.

C0069.label: Economic precarity and household strain during the Depression
C0069.definition: The speaker frames the Depression era as financially rough, marked by the husband's irregular income and the out-of-pocket burden of educating children in parochial school.
C0069.model_rationale: These sentences together articulate the analytic concept of Depression-era household precarity, with school tuition costs as a salient pressure point — not merely a chronology of events.

C0070.label: Long-tenure meatpacking labor as economic mainstay
C0070.definition: Sustained, nearly three-decade employment in a packing house doing physically demanding meat-cutting work served as the household's economic anchor.
C0070.model_rationale: The duration ('close to thirty years') and the specificity of the cutting/sausage work elevate this beyond a job mention to a concept of working-class industrial tenure.

C0071.label: Intergenerational workplace co-participation in the plant
C0071.definition: Mother and daughter worked in the same packing house, illustrating the transmission of working-class industrial labor across generations within immigrant families.
C0071.model_rationale: Mitzi's entry into the same plant and progression from floor to smokehouse is coded as a concept of kin-based labor transmission rather than as a one-off itinerary step.

C0072.label: Health-driven interruption and exit from industrial work
C0072.definition: Physical and medical vulnerability repeatedly disrupts or terminates packing-house employment for both the speaker and her daughter.
C0072.model_rationale: The recurring motif of surgeries and medical forced exit shows that bodily health, not choice, structured the arc of factory work — an analytic point about the body as a labor constraint.

C0073.label: Naturalization and habitual civic participation
C0073.definition: Acquiring U.S. citizenship in 1932 and voting in every subsequent election constitutes a practiced, ritualized form of civic belonging.
C0073.model_rationale: The pairing of naturalization date with continuous voting encodes a concept of citizenship-as-practice rather than a mere biographical fact.

C0074.label: Chosen American identity and affective belonging
C0074.definition: The speaker's expressed happiness at returning from Europe and declaration that 'This is my country' reveal an emotionally invested, self-chosen national identification despite immigrant origins.
C0074.model_rationale: The affective framing — joy at return, emphatic ownership ('This is my country') — points to an underlying claim of belonging that contrasts with her immigrant status, warranting a latent code.

C0075.label: Staged pioneer migration with father as advance party
C0075.definition: A migration pattern in which one parent (here the father) departs a year ahead of the rest of the family to establish a foothold before bringing them over.
C0075.model_rationale: The 'year before' phrasing and the sequence of father-then-family depicts a classic pioneer/preparatory migration strategy rather than a single joint departure.

C0076.label: Migration timing tied to family formation
C0076.definition: The mother’s arrival in England is positioned as coinciding with the birth of the children, suggesting migration was timed around family founding.
C0076.model_rationale: Frames the move from Warsaw to the East End as part of establishing a household rather than as an isolated economic decision, even though the interviewer questions this sequencing.

C0077.label: Pre-migration occupational continuity as economic foothold
C0077.definition: Carrying a homeland trade into the new country provided an immediate basis for the family business and household stability.
C0077.model_rationale: The confirmation that the mother was already a baker in Poland and the opening of the bakery on the Lower East Side illustrates the analytic idea of transferable skills underwriting immigrant economic establishment.

C0078.label: Onomastic transformation across the life course
C0078.definition: A change of personal name from the name borne in childhood or at emigration to the name carried in later life, marking biographical transitions such as immigration or marriage.
C0078.model_rationale: The subject is introduced by her married/later name (Minnie Rodwin) but the interviewer explicitly recovers the originary name (Mary Floun) borne at the time of emigration, evidencing a recognized shift in personal name across the life course.

C0079.label: Divergence between registered date and biographical date
C0079.definition: Instances in which an officially recorded date for a life event differs from the date recalled or reported by the subject or family, registering a gap between bureaucratic documentation and lived/familial memory.
C0079.model_rationale: The interviewer's explicit prompting that the subject 'has two' birth dates and the confirmation of a Christmas-morning birth against a February 9th registration marks a documented case of official-versus-biographical date divergence.

C0080.label: Maternal oral tradition as biographical authority
C0080.definition: The practice of treating a mother's remembered account as the primary source for details of one's own origins, including specifics absent from official records and supplying the only access to early-life information.
C0080.model_rationale: The subject's knowledge of her precise birth hour ('six o'clock in the morning') and of her mother's bakery business in London is sourced specifically through the mother's verbal account, positioning maternal speech as the authoritative archive for unrecorded biography.

C0081.label: Ritualized anchoring of subject in time, place, name, and institutional context
C0081.definition: The conventional opening move of an oral history interview in which the interviewer fixes the subject's identity by eliciting current age, originary name, location, birth date, and the conducting institution.
C0081.model_rationale: The interviewer's repeated checks of age, place, institutional affiliation (National Park Service), and request to restate biographical data for the tape exemplify the standardized anchoring ritual by which oral history sessions authenticate the subject and situate the testimony.

C0082.label: Fragmented early childhood memory and the limits of recollection
C0082.definition: The speaker's pre-verbal-era memories (from age three and a half) surface only as discrete fragments, requiring active probing by the interviewer to elicit any descriptive content at all.
C0082.model_rationale: The repeated interviewer prompts and the speaker's halting, 'what do you call it?' retrieval show that very early memories are sparse, requiring scaffolding rather than flowing narrative.

C0083.label: Interpersonal encounter as a vivid early memory anchor
C0083.definition: A discrete, dialogically preserved episode with a neighbor functions as one of the few fully realized memory fragments from this earliest period, suggesting emotionally charged social interactions anchor recollection more than physical settings.
C0083.model_rationale: The encounter is recalled in quoted dialogue ('Mary, Mary,' 'I just want to tell you not to make so—so—noise'), indicating it is preserved at a sensory/conversational level rather than as abstract description.

C0084.label: Ethnic composition of the neighborhood as salient memory marker
C0084.definition: The Irish identity of the neighbor across the street is singled out and preserved in the speaker's memory, indicating that ethnic/immigrant character of the surrounding community was a notable feature of the remembered landscape.
C0084.model_rationale: The redundancy of naming the neighbor's nationality ('She was Irish') and its placement as a defining detail implies the speaker's surroundings were ethnically marked in ways that stood out for a memory operating with very few available details.

C0085.label: Misrecognition and the construction of childhood identity
C0085.definition: The neighbor addresses the child by a name that is not hers, revealing either unfamiliarity with the family or the child's liminal, not-fully-known status in the neighborhood, with implications for how identity is socially conferred in early childhood.
C0085.model_rationale: Beyond a one-off name confusion, the misnaming signals the family's recent arrival or marginal social position in the street, framing the child as someone not yet known to the adult neighbors.

C0086.label: Childhood self-defense against perceived unfair criticism
C0086.definition: The speaker's preserved protest ('I was quiet') against the noise accusation shows that the memory is filtered through a residual sense of grievance, with the child remembered as defending her own conduct against adult reproach.
C0086.model_rationale: The affective charge of the episode is preserved as a small injustice remembered, an analytic pattern in which early memories of authority figures often carry a self-justifying layer.

C0087.label: Material culture as memory anchor and class marker
C0087.definition: A specific domestic object (the icebox) operates simultaneously as a concrete retrieval cue for otherwise inaccessible early memory and as a symbol of the family's modest socioeconomic distinction from neighbors who lacked such an appliance.
C0087.model_rationale: The interviewer and speaker collaboratively build the icebox into a shared marker, repeatedly contrasting it with what 'a lot of people didn't have,' showing how physical possessions do double duty as memory aid and class signifier.

C0088.label: Tension between documentary record and embodied memory
C0088.definition: The interviewer's invocation of the birth certificate as a known fact about the house, juxtaposed with the speaker's inability to describe that house, foregrounds the gap between official documentation and personal, embodied recollection.
C0088.model_rationale: The exchange makes explicit that the speaker's factual address can be verified on paper while remaining experientially blank, a methodological and analytic tension central to oral history of pre-verbal or pre-migratory childhood.

C0089.label: Co-location of family enterprise and household
C0089.definition: The family lived at the same premises as the bakery on Commercial Road, blending domestic and commercial space.
C0089.model_rationale: A common pattern among immigrant small-business households where home and shop coincide; the family did not merely work at the bakery but resided there.

C0090.label: Eastern European origins of both parents
C0090.definition: Both the mother and the father traced their origins to Poland, with the mother specifically from Warsaw, situating the family within a Polish-Jewish diaspora.
C0090.model_rationale: Establishes the transnational migration background of the parental generation and hints at endogamous marriage patterns within a shared sending community.

C0091.label: Folk etymology of family name as cultural marker
C0091.definition: The mother’s maiden name Vivyeika is glossed by the interviewee as meaning 'squirrel,' preserving a piece of linguistic heritage.
C0091.model_rationale: The interviewee flags the name as 'funny' and offers a vernacular translation, signalling that the unusual Eastern European surname carries cultural meaning for him.

C0092.label: Paternal military service
C0092.definition: The father’s army background is established as a salient biographical feature of his life before settling in England.
C0092.model_rationale: Repeated back-and-forth identification of the father as a soldier makes this a key biographical anchor, even though specific details are not supplied.

C0093.label: Memory gaps in biographical recall
C0093.definition: The interviewee repeatedly signals inability to remember specific details of the family’s pre-migration life, particularly around origins and the father’s army service.
C0093.model_rationale: Phrases like 'I forget all this' and 'I knew all these things but the—' reveal how late-life oral history is shaped by forgotten specifics, especially regarding the country of origin and the father’s military biography.

C0094.label: Family assembly through transnational migration and remarriage
C0094.definition: The parental household was constituted across borders and through sequential unions, including the mother's prior widowhood and remarriage to the father after his return.
C0094.model_rationale: Captures how the parents' marriage was embedded in a chain of transnational movement and marital recomposition rather than treating the prior marriage or the return as isolated itinerary steps.

C0095.label: Chronic in-law antagonism
C0095.definition: Persistent harassment from the mother-in-law directed at the mother, disrupting the marital relationship.
C0095.model_rationale: Names the ongoing interpersonal pattern rather than the single instance of conflict.

C0096.label: Displaced grief as moral accusation
C0096.definition: The mother-in-law reframes her son's death as caused by the mother's 'waiting,' transmuting bereavement into blame against the new wife.
C0096.model_rationale: Goes beyond the surface antagonism to identify the underlying causal narrative in which grief is displaced onto the woman the deceased was waiting for.

C0097.label: Geographic relocation as escape from family conflict
C0097.definition: Moving to a new city as a strategy to remove the family from a hostile in-law's orbit.
C0097.model_rationale: Frames the move to London as a coping strategy in response to harassment rather than as a neutral itinerary step.

C0098.label: Immigrant self-employment via small trade
C0098.definition: Opening a small family business (bakery) as the economic foundation of the reconstituted household in the new city.
C0098.model_rationale: Abstracts the bakery opening into a broader analytic category of how refugee/immigrant households established livelihoods, rather than logging it as a one-off plot point.

C0099.label: Caregiving-capacity threshold as migration filter
C0099.definition: Children were left behind because the mother judged she could not adequately care for them, framing who migrates as a function of perceived ability to provide for them.
C0099.model_rationale: Rodwin attributes the separation to maternal fear of incapacity to care for the two sisters, revealing an implicit calculus whereby caregiving capacity, not preference, gates which children move.

C0100.label: Grandmother as kin-keeper in country of origin
C0100.definition: Extended kin (grandmother) assumed the parental caregiving role for children who remained in Poland, enabling the parents' departure for London.
C0100.model_rationale: The grandmother functions structurally as the substitute parent for the left-behind children; her role is the mechanism that makes disaggregated migration feasible.

C0101.label: Photographic mediation of absent kin
C0101.definition: Familial relationships with members not co-present during early life are known and accessed through photographs rather than through embodied memory.
C0101.model_rationale: Rodwin's claim that he 'just' knows his grandmother 'from pictures' signals that diaspora kinship is partially constituted by visual media standing in for lived contact.

C0102.label: Birth-order self-location within the diaspora sibling cohort
C0102.definition: Personal identity is anchored by position among siblings born in the host country rather than by a single global birth order within the whole family.
C0102.model_rationale: 'Next to the youngest' locates the speaker within the England-born subset of the nine, showing that the post-migration cohort, not the whole sibling set, supplies the reference group for ordinal identity.

C0103.label: Planned bounded separation between parents and left-behind children
C0103.definition: The parting of the two sisters from their mother was framed as a temporary arrangement with a defined endpoint rather than as an open-ended rupture.
C0103.model_rationale: 'Just till one was seven years old' encodes an intended horizon of reunion, presenting the separation as a managed interval rather than as definitive loss; the phrase's casual brevity contrasts with the gravity such separations could carry.

C0104.label: Military conscription recall as emigration push factor
C0104.definition: The father's recall to the Russian Army functioned as the precipitating threat that made the family choose to leave Europe for the United States.
C0104.model_rationale: The narrator and interviewer explicitly identify the army recall as the reason for departure, establishing conscription as a causal driver rather than mere background context.

C0105.label: Family roster that preserves the memory of a deceased sibling
C0105.definition: The enumeration of children includes Yettie, who died, showing that oral family histories integrate lost members into the remembered order of births.
C0105.model_rationale: Yettie's inclusion in the birth order and the narrator's terse announcement of her death mark how family reckonings hold space for the deceased as a structural part of the sibling list.

C0106.label: Confused postwar military obligations across shifting borders
C0106.definition: The 'underground' Russian Army to which the father was recalled reflects how redrawn East European borders left civilian populations subject to ambiguous conscription claims from successor states.
C0106.model_rationale: The interviewer's prompting ('Maybe it was the Russian Army,' 'those borders changed a lot') and the narrator's 'underground' qualifier expose the geopolitical indeterminacy underlying the recall, a latent condition of interwar Jewish migration.

C0107.label: Ethnic/religious segregation as organizing principle in transit
C0107.definition: Ships and other transit infrastructures assigned passengers to designated sections by ethnicity or religion, here a Jewish section, functioning as a form of diaspora community continuity at sea.
C0107.model_rationale: The interviewer's surprise at the existence of a Jewish section suggests it was a structural feature of steerage travel that institutionalized communal clustering.

C0108.label: Steerage as cramped, shared, low-status accommodation
C0108.definition: Passengers in the lowest deck ('bottom of the ship') shared crowded quarters with bunk beds, marking class-based spatial segregation.
C0108.model_rationale: Describes the material conditions of third-class travel and the shared, dense living arrangement.

C0109.label: Children performing for tips among passengers
C0109.definition: Young passengers entertained fellow travelers through dancing and singing, receiving thrown coins as informal compensation in a liminal, unregulated economy.
C0109.model_rationale: Captures the informal child-labor/economy that arose in steerage spaces of entertainment and tipping.

C0110.label: Maternal moral gatekeeping of children's earnings
C0110.definition: The mother acted as a normative authority figure, restricting children's access to money earned through informal performance to uphold propriety or dignity.
C0110.model_rationale: Beyond literal refusal, this signals a parental stance on class respectability and protecting children from informal street-economy norms.

C0111.label: Diaspora networks reproducing kosher practice at sea
C0111.definition: Co-religionist crew members (here a chef) leveraged prior acquaintance and shared observance to smuggle or provision kosher food to passengers, sustaining religious dietary law outside the home.
C0111.model_rationale: Shows how Jewish networks operated institutionally and informally across borders to maintain religious practice in transit.

C0112.label: Iconic arrival imagery as memory anchor
C0112.definition: The Statue of Liberty sighting functions as a vivid, enduring collective memory marker of arrival in America, often recalled alongside other sensory impressions.
C0112.model_rationale: The rush to see the Statue of Liberty is a canonical immigrant-arrival motif anchoring episodic memory of the journey's endpoint.

C0113.label: Child vulnerability and informal caregiving in transit
C0113.definition: A fellow passenger intervened when the child soiled himself, undressing, cleaning, and redressing him, illustrating how parenting and caregiving roles were diffused among strangers during travel.
C0113.model_rationale: Highlights the precariousness of child supervision in steerage and the communal caregiving that filled gaps left by overstretched parents.

C0114.label: Cross-national linguistic identity in parent-child bond
C0114.definition: Persistence of a British/English term of address for the father ('Daddy') over the Yiddish-influenced 'Papa,' signaling the formative influence of the London transit stop on the child's identity.
C0114.model_rationale: The narrator's own explanation that 'in London it was Daddy' frames the term of address as a marker of the prior national context, an analytic point about layered national identity rather than a simple vocabulary item.

C0115.label: Wife as established tradesperson in immigrant economy
C0115.definition: The wife brought a craft (baking) from the homeland, and the husband shifted into her trade, inverting the typical expectation of male occupational continuity.
C0115.model_rationale: The exchange in which the husband, a tailor in Poland, becomes a baker 'from your mother' encodes a gendered pattern of skill transfer within the family enterprise, not just a biographical fact.

C0116.label: Childhood sensory memory of pre-automotive urban landscape
C0116.definition: The horse-drawn delivery wagons register as a vivid first impression of the Lower East Side, marking the city as still partially animal-powered in the child's experience.
C0116.model_rationale: The wagons are recalled not as itinerary but as a salient sensory marker of a transitional urban era, an analytic point about how childhood memory preserves the texture of a city before mechanization.

C0117.label: Tenement housing pattern on the Lower East Side
C0117.definition: Residence in a four-story walkup tenement at a specific LES address, typical of early-twentieth-century immigrant settlement.
C0117.model_rationale: The precise address, walkup configuration, and identification of the building as a tenement point to a recurring residential form of immigrant family life, not merely a personal address.

C0118.label: Selective childhood memory of the migration journey
C0118.definition: The child retains vivid images of the destination neighborhood but no memory of the Ellis Island processing, illustrating how early trauma or bureaucratic moments are filtered out of recollection.
C0118.model_rationale: The contrast between complete amnesia for Ellis Island and detailed sensory recall of the LES suggests an analytic pattern in which arrival experience is selectively preserved, with the institutional processing point elided in favor of neighborhood impressions.

C0119.label: Communal and pay-per-use tenement infrastructure
C0119.definition: Basic building facilities (toilets, gas) were either shared between apartments or required coin insertion per use, marking the material conditions of recent-arrival housing on the Lower East Side.
C0119.model_rationale: Both the hall-shared bathroom and quarter-fed gas meters are described as routine features of the Essex Street apartment, signaling a housing stock organized around collective or metered access rather than private provision.

C0120.label: Institutional sanctioning of children's domestic labor over schooling
C0120.definition: School authorities told the immigrant mother to keep her older children home to help her, revealing an institutional tolerance of child contribution to household survival that overrode routine expectations of school enrollment.
C0120.model_rationale: The quoted directive ('Let them stay home and help you') from school personnel frames children's domestic labor as an acceptable alternative to attendance, illustrating how gatekeepers mediated immigrant children's access to formal education.

C0121.label: Prior educational socialization in country of origin as a resettlement resource
C0121.definition: The speaker's earlier school attendance from age three in London provided a foundational familiarity with classroom routines and knowledge that preceded and eased her entry into American schooling.
C0121.model_rationale: The interviewer's repeated underscoring that the speaker 'already knew a lot of things' foregrounds pre-migration schooling as a portable asset brought into the resettlement experience, distinct from the brothers' situation.

C0122.label: Neighborhood amenity proximity as texture of immigrant daily life
C0122.definition: Walkable, immediately accessible local businesses such as the bakery across the street, paired with a positive affective recollection ('we had a nice time'), situate daily routines within a dense, service-rich immigrant neighborhood.
C0122.model_rationale: The bakery's proximity and the speaker's warm summary gesture toward the everyday comforts of the block, capturing how proximate amenities anchored everyday life on the Lower East Side.

C0123.label: Intergenerational family labor in ethnic enterprise
C0123.definition: Children contributing labor to a family-owned business, especially around culturally specific or seasonal tasks.
C0123.model_rationale: Rodwin describes helping her parents in the bakery, with holiday-specific work of decorating macaroons with almonds and cherries, showing child participation in a family trade tied to cultural foodways.

C0124.label: Spousal meeting through shared trade
C0124.definition: Romantic pairing originating in or through the same occupational sector or workplace.
C0124.model_rationale: Husband was a bakery delivery driver, illustrating how the bakery industry itself connected the couple.

C0125.label: Reflexive valuation of family cohesion
C0125.definition: Retrospective framing of family unity as a defining and valued feature of life.
C0125.model_rationale: When prompted to look back, Rodwin immediately names 'always together' as the salient quality, signaling family solidarity as a central, nostalgic theme rather than a stated plot point.

C0126.label: Large-family childbearing pattern
C0126.definition: Bearing many children, characteristic of early-twentieth-century immigrant working-class households.
C0126.model_rationale: Six children indexes the demographic/family-formation norm of her cohort and class.

C0127.label: Continuity of family enterprise across residential transitions
C0127.definition: Carrying the family business forward when relocating rather than abandoning the trade.
C0127.model_rationale: The family operated bakeries in both the Lower East Side and Rockaway Beach, treating the firm as a portable anchor of household life.

C0128.label: Intra-urban relocation from immigrant enclave after long residence
C0128.definition: Departure from a dense immigrant neighborhood to another part of the metropolitan area after an extended period of settlement.
C0128.model_rationale: Roughly fifteen years on the East Side followed by a move to Rockaway Beach maps onto the classic immigrant trajectory of leaving the initial enclave for a more suburban destination.

C0129.label: Fusion of family name and business identity
C0129.definition: The family surname functioning simultaneously as the firm's name, indexing the inseparability of household and enterprise in the immigrant economy.
C0129.model_rationale: The bakery is 'Floun's'—the maiden name—revealing that the kin unit and the firm were effectively one institution.

C0130.label: Bakery as immigrant kin gathering site
C0130.definition: The family business functioned as a recurring meeting point for relatives, illustrating how immigrant-owned enterprises served as anchors of kin networks within ethnic enclaves.
C0130.model_rationale: Multiple sentences describe relatives regularly coming to the father's bakery on Fridays and gathering at the home, indicating the business doubled as a hub for kin ties rather than purely a commercial site.

C0131.label: Spousal-contingent female migration
C0131.definition: A pattern in which a woman's migration was conditional on her husband's decision, reflecting couple-based migration norms in which female mobility followed male partners.
C0131.model_rationale: The mother is reported to have wanted to come to America only because the father was going, surfacing the dependent framing of female migration in this household.

C0132.label: Positive retrospective evaluation of migration
C0132.definition: A satisfied or favorable after-the-fact assessment of the migration experience and its outcomes for the immigrant generation.
C0132.model_rationale: Both the narrator's own summary ('it was nice') and the reported contentment of the parents convey an unambiguously favorable retrospective stance toward the migration.

C0133.label: Second-generation geographic dispersal
C0133.definition: The scattering of adult immigrant-descendant children across distant regions of the host country, characteristic of post-migration family trajectories.
C0133.model_rationale: Daughters settled near L.A. and in Atlanta, exemplifying the regional dispersion of the second generation away from the original immigrant enclave.

C0134.label: Fading autobiographical memory in late life
C0134.definition: Uncertainty or gaps in recall of formative life events associated with advanced age.
C0134.model_rationale: The narrator's inability to confirm whether she visited Ellis Island is read as a marker of autobiographical memory limits at ninety years old, rather than as biographical fact.

C0135.label: Understated contentment as evaluative register
C0135.definition: A recurring tone of modest, undramatic satisfaction that characterizes the speaker's evaluations across multiple life domains and across time.
C0135.model_rationale: Repeated uses of 'nice,' 'all right,' and 'they were all right' across past relatives, the migration experience, and present life suggest a stable affective register of low-key contentment rather than enthusiastic endorsement.

C0136.label: Generational continuity through descendants
C0136.definition: The presence of grandchildren as material evidence of an ongoing family lineage extending beyond the immigrant generation.
C0136.model_rationale: The mention and offer to display photographs of grandchildren mark the third generation as a tangible continuation of the lineage established by the immigrant parents.

dp-40-grande-m.central_concept: In this account, the child's emotional world at the moment of departure is structured entirely by the anticipation of reuniting with her absent father, with awareness of what was being left behind arriving only in retrospect.

subthemes.claim: At ten years old, leaving is felt as forward movement toward paternal reunion, not as loss or farewell.
In adulthood, the cultural and linguistic costs of that departure become legible as losses the child could not have anticipated.
The same childhood vantage that produced peripheral awareness of geopolitical change also produced peripheral awareness of what was being given up.
The narrator's adult emotional response diverges from her mother's persistent longing, marking a generational split in how the homeland is felt.

dp-40-grande-m.falsified_if: The narrator explicitly recalled grief, missing friends, or a sense of farewell as her dominant orientation at the moment of departure.

dp-40-grande-m.central_concept: In this account, the mother-daughter pair's capacity to act during emigration is structurally dependent on diasporic male intermediaries, with the mother carrying the bureaucratic labor and the child remaining legally subsumed.

subthemes.claim: The mother bears the bureaucratic burden of securing emigration approvals alone, traversing multiple courts and offices in the final month.
The child's travel authorization is legally bundled into the mother's passport, marking her as a dependent rather than an agent in the process.

Male diaspora intermediaries intervene at points of structural vulnerability — a returnee who speaks American restores stolen clothes, and a previously-arrived fellow passenger advocates for an upgraded berth.
The journey itself is composed of repeated constrained transfers — multi-port shipping, institutional food, medical inspection — over which the travelers exercise little control.
Family emigration is staged rather than unitary: brothers are bound by military service, and a brother's death reshapes which siblings can leave and when.
The absent father anchors the process from the destination side through letters and prepaid tickets, coordinating a transnational division of labor.
Communal provisioning among co-traveling mothers partially substitutes for the missing male provisioner of another family in the group.

dp-40-grande-m.falsified_if: A stage of the journey is navigated by the mother-daughter pair through their own initiative without male intermediary intervention, or the child independently pursues paperwork.

dp-40-grande-m.central_concept: In this account, resettlement is structurally mediated by co-ethnic networks of regional origin — co-migrant travel, company housing alongside similar families, endogamous marriage, and chain-migration growth — making incorporation a collective project rather than an individual arrival.

subthemes.claim: Families from the same European area travel, transfer, and ultimately resettle together in the same American town.
Initial housing places the family beside co-ethnic neighbors, and peer children mediate the narrator's first English acquisition.
Within that networked setting, the narrator is rapidly enrolled and placed above expected grade, with non-verbal schoolwork enabling early academic footholds.
Marriage within the shared regional origin reinforces the network rather than dispersing from it.
The ethnic enclave sustains itself by attracting and absorbing successive newcomers, who prefer to integrate within the existing group.
The father's itinerant movement among mining sites, and his eventual health-driven relocation, intersect with the same kin-based infrastructure that organized arrival.

dp-40-grande-m.falsified_if: The narrator describes resettling into a neighborhood without co-ethnic presence or forming her primary ties outside the regional origin network.

dp-40-grande-m.central_concept: In this account, the narrator's memory of the home village is anchored to an integrated human-animal dwelling managed by the mother in the father's long absence, set within a landscape materially inscribed by imperial rule and wartime state coercion.

subthemes.claim: The father's longstanding presence in America leaves the mother as the de facto operator of the rural household and farm.
The peasant house and barn are built as a single structure with animals immediately adjacent to domestic rooms, a layout the narrator describes as historically typical.
Livestock are remembered as childhood companions integrated into daily domestic life, not as utility objects.
The seasonal slaughter-and-preservation cycle organizes the household year, with the meat cured, weighted, and smoked for summer use.
Children are obligated to participate in slaughter labor, performing necessary but unpleasant tasks like catching blood for blood sausage.

Imperial rule is materially inscribed in the village through the visible presence of an Austrian elite occupying the local castle and through the imposed German language layered over Slovene as the official tongue.
Wartime state coercion reshapes domestic space, requiring households to surrender rooms to refugees and imposing consumer restrictions on the civilian population.
Wartime scarcity is met through rural-urban barter across the river and through strategic adaptation to environmental hazards such as hospital contamination of shared water.

dp-40-grande-m.falsified_if: The narrator describes her father as a present figure in the home village or describes the mother as peripheral to farm operations.

dp-40-grande-m.central_concept: In this account, belonging to America is anchored in active civic practice — citizenship, habitual voting, and explicit declaration — rather than in ancestral place, and is sustained across the life course through stable residence, long-tenure industrial work, and a retrospective distance from a homeland that has politically transformed and war-traumatized.

subthemes.claim: Citizenship acquired in 1932 and ritualized voting in every subsequent election constitute a practiced, ongoing form of civic belonging.
The narrator explicitly claims America as her country and expresses affective happiness at returning from Europe, marking identification as chosen rather than inherited.
Long-tenure meatpacking labor — nearly three decades — anchors economic life, and the work is transmitted to the next generation within the same immigrant household.
Settlement in Denver is uninterrupted from 1926 onward, and the homeland property is formally transferred to the brother, finalizing severance from the place of origin.
The homeland has become politically unrecognizable — shifting from Austria-Hungary to Yugoslavia — and is associated in memory with war trauma suffered by relatives who remained.
The fear of a second European war overrides the mother's wish to return, consolidating permanent emigration.
Persistent transnational ties — multiple return visits — coexist with chosen American identification rather than displacing it.
The mother's labor is redefined after emigration from managing agricultural animals to domestic housework, marking her adjustment into a different female work order.
Generational divergence in diasporic longing — the mother's intense homesickness against the narrator's comparatively muted response — is named in the interview.
German, the official language of the imperial homeland, fades from use after emigration and is later regretted as a cultural loss.
The arrival in Colorado coincides with the onset of severe Depression-era hardship, framing American economic life as demanding but ultimately incorporable.

dp-40-grande-m.falsified_if: The narrator identifies primarily with her country of origin or expresses a sustained desire to return permanently to Europe.

ei-845-rodwin.falsified_if: If a second account showed the narrator resettling without recourse to pre-existing co-ethnic infrastructure — no kin, no ethnic enclave, no ethnically-coded employment — and incorporating as an isolated individual.
