ei-845-rodwin.central_concept: In this account, transatlantic emigration is initiated by the father's recall to the military of a successor state following border changes, producing a pioneer-father pattern in which the father departs alone roughly a year ahead of the family and sends prepaid tickets for their later passage — inverting the pattern in which the mother is the primary actor.

subthemes.claim: The father's recall to what the narrator describes as an 'underground' Russian Army — reflecting confusion of conscription claims from successor states after border changes — functions as the precipitating push factor for transatlantic emigration.

The father departs roughly a year ahead of the wife and children, opens a downtown Manhattan bakery, and then summons the family with prepaid tickets, while the mother waits in London contingent on his prior decision.

ei-845-rodwin.falsified_if: If a second account showed the entire family departing together without a pioneer father, or showed that economic opportunity rather than a conscription threat was the stated precipitating cause of the father's earlier solo departure.

ei-845-rodwin.central_concept: In this account, the mother's pre-migration baking trade — not the father's tailoring — is the portable economic infrastructure that carries the family from Poland to London to the Lower East Side, with the husband eventually entering the wife's trade and the family surname fusing household, firm, and kin network into a single structure.

subthemes.claim: The mother's baking trade, established in Poland and replicated first in London (Commercial Road) and then in New York (Essex Street), provides occupational continuity across three countries and a co-located business-and-residence pattern.
The father, identified in Poland as a tailor, enters the mother's trade and becomes a baker in the new country, inverting the conventional pattern of male occupational continuity.

The family business carries the family surname ('Floun's Bakery') and operates as a kin gathering site where relatives assemble on Fridays, fusing the firm, the household, and the co-ethnic network into a single structure, and this enterprise travels with the family to Rockaway Beach after fifteen years on the Lower East Side.

ei-845-rodwin.falsified_if: If a second account showed the father retaining his pre-migration trade in the new country, or showed the firm named by something other than the family surname, or showed the household and business geographically separated.

ei-845-rodwin.central_concept: In this account, family emigration is not a unitary act but a selective one governed by perceived caregiving capacity: two sisters remain in Poland with the grandmother because the mother judges she cannot adequately care for them, and the timing of the mother's own move from Poland to London is positioned as coinciding with the birth of her children.

subthemes.claim: Two sisters are left behind in Poland with the grandmother because the mother judges she cannot adequately care for them, framing who migrates as a function of who can be provided for.

The grandmother, herself only known to the narrator through photographs, is positioned as the kin-keeper who enables the parental departure, with relationships to absent kin accessed through images rather than embodied memory.
The parting of the two sisters is framed as a temporary arrangement with a defined endpoint ('till one was seven years old'), not as open-ended rupture, even though it becomes permanent in practice.

The mother's movement from Poland to London is positioned as coinciding with the birth of her children, suggesting that migration is timed around family formation rather than pursued independently of it.

ei-845-rodwin.falsified_if: If a second account showed all children migrating together regardless of the mother's caregiving capacity, or showed the parents' departure from a country of origin with no extended kin available to absorb the children left behind.

ei-845-rodwin.central_concept: In this account, emigration at age three and a half produces a memory architecture in which recollection is fragmentary, anchored by vivid sensory and interpersonal fragments and supplemented by maternal oral tradition rather than documentary records, with biographical dates sometimes diverging from officially registered ones.

subthemes.claim: Memory of the pre-verbal-era emigration is fragmentary, requiring active interviewer probing to surface any descriptive content at all, and the speaker herself signals the limits of recall at multiple points — forenames, the father's army service, Ellis Island.

Specific sensory and interpersonal fragments — the icebox the family owned when most neighbors did not, the Irish neighbor who called 'Mary, Mary' to scold her for noise — function as memory anchors more durable than physical settings the speaker cannot describe, and the neighborhood's ethnic composition is preserved as a salient marker.

Iconic arrival imagery (the Statue of Liberty, horse-drawn delivery wagons) marks the destination vividly, while Ellis Island processing is forgotten — showing that salient sensory and collective-symbolic content survives in memory while bureaucratic procedures do not.

The mother's oral tradition — 'my mother used to say' — rather than documentary records, serves as the primary biographical authority for the narrator's own origins, with the birth certificate invoked by the interviewer rather than recalled by the speaker.

Biographical and officially registered dates sometimes diverge (Christmas morning birth vs. February 9 registration), registering a persistent gap between bureaucratic documentation and lived/familial memory.
The family roster recited in the interview preserves the memory of a deceased sibling (Yettie), integrating lost members into the remembered order of births.

ei-845-rodwin.falsified_if: If a second account from a similarly young emigrant showed continuous, detailed autobiographical memory of the journey and origin, or showed the speaker relying primarily on documents rather than maternal oral tradition for biographical facts.
